source.content:
Marcus Reed: perf bench results for cache telemetry are in. TL;DR: counters are basically free, histograms + miss-reason breakdown need sampling to stay under our p95 budget.

Marcus Reed: ran on A100 80GB + H100 80GB, 3 workloads:
- chat (multi-turn, streaming)
- summarization (long prompt, short decode)
- rerank-ish (short prompt, medium decode)

Marcus Reed: baseline vs instrumentation:
- counters only (hit/miss/evict gauges): +0.1–0.3% p95 (noise)
- counters + histograms @ 100%: +2.8% p95 (chat), +1.9% p95 (summ), +2.2% p95 (rerank)
- counters + histograms @ 10%: +0.6–0.9% p95
- counters + histograms @ 1%: +0.1–0.3% p95

Marcus Reed: also checked CPU overhead in runtime process (profiling): hottest path is histogram bucket calc + label assembly; sampling early helps a lot.

Marcus Reed: doc: https://drive.google.com/.../cache-telemetry-perf-overhead-bench-results (same numbers as the sheet i pasted in standup)

perf-canary-bot: perf-canary run: cache-telemetry-overhead-canary
status=PASS
p95_delta=+0.7%
p99_delta=+0.9%
config=hist_sample=0.10 trace_sample=0.00
commit=redwood@3f2a9c1

Omar El-Sayed: nice. the +2-3% at 100% matches what i was seeing locally. i’m comfortable w/ counters always-on.

Samir Qureshi: question: when you say “histograms” is that both prefix reuse depth + wasted prefill, or just prefix reuse?

Marcus Reed: both. prefix reuse depth + reused_tokens buckets + wasted_prefill_tokens buckets. miss_reason breakdown is counters but adds tag work; still cheap relative to hist.

Sofia Ivanova: +0.6–0.9% at 10% seems fine, but do we need 10% in prod? SRE asked for “stable enough to alert on”.

Nadia Rahman: from telemetry side: 10% for histograms might still be $$$ at hosted volume. also cardinality guards will drop some labels anyway; we should assume sampling is the main cost control.

Caleb Johnson: +1 on cost. ingestion can handle it, but we’ll pay for it. if we pick 10% by default, we need explicit guardrails so someone doesn’t turn on extra labels + 10% and melt the budget.

Omar El-Sayed: what if we do:
- counters: 100%
- histograms: 1% default in Hosted, 10% in Dedicated/Private (lower volume, more need for per-route debugging)
- traces (span annotations): 0% by default, opt-in 0.1% when debugging

Julian Moore: from scheduler perspective, 1% is fine for distribution shape. the alerts are gonna key off hit-rate counters, not histograms. hist is for diagnosis (prompt bucket shifts / reuse depth collapse).

Mateo Alvarez: agree w/ Omar’s split. we need “boring defaults” for Hosted. Dedicated customers will ask for richer data when something regresses.

Connor O'Brien: operationally: can we express that split as config by deployment_mode? i’d rather not have to ship separate binaries.

Omar El-Sayed: yep. PR-18429 adds flags + env overrides. we can do `cache_metrics_hist_sample_rate` by mode.

Samir Qureshi: i’m slightly worried 1% for wasted_prefill could be too spiky if we slice by prompt_length_bucket. but if counters cover “wasted_prefill_total” too, we can keep alerting stable.

Nadia Rahman: recommendation: counters at 100% for:
- cache_hit_tokens / cache_miss_tokens (or whatever final names are after PR-18431)
- wasted_prefill_tokens_total
and hist only for bucketed views.

Caleb Johnson: +1. also please don’t sample counters; sampled counters + rate() math always becomes a support nightmare.

Sofia Ivanova: what about miss_reason counters? 100% too?

Marcus Reed: miss_reason counters 100% looked fine (+0.2–0.4% p95 in my run). most of the cost was hist bucket computations.

Omar El-Sayed: ok proposal for default prod:
- counters (hit/miss tokens, reqs, evictions, pressure gauge, miss_reason): 1.0
- histograms (reuse depth, reused tokens, wasted prefill bucketed): Hosted 0.01, Dedicated 0.10, Private 0.10
- traces: 0.0

Mateo Alvarez: :thumbsup: from me. keep a documented “debug preset” that bumps hist to 0.25 + traces to 0.001 for 1h.

Nadia Rahman: +1 but add “cap” in ingestion so even if someone sets 1.0, we clamp at 0.25 unless allowlisted.

Caleb Johnson: i can add that clamp on the ingestion side (and log a warning + metric).

Connor O'Brien: works for rollout plan too. we can canary Hosted w/ 0.02 first, then step down if cost surprises.

Marcus Reed: cool. i’ll update the perf thread + drop a note on the rollout checklist. also: on H100 numbers are slightly better (hist @10% was ~0.5% p95), but i wouldn’t bank on that.

Omar El-Sayed: can you paste the exact config you used for the perf-canary bot run? want to match apples-to-apples.

Marcus Reed: yup:
```
CACHE_METRICS_ENABLE=1
CACHE_METRICS_COUNTER_SAMPLE_RATE=1.0
CACHE_METRICS_HIST_SAMPLE_RATE=0.10
CACHE_TRACE_SAMPLE_RATE=0.0
CACHE_METRICS_LABEL_SET=restricted
```

Sofia Ivanova: “restricted” label set = no per-tenant, right?

Omar El-Sayed: correct. route/model/version/deployment_mode/region + prompt_length_bucket. no tenant_id, no request_id.

Mateo Alvarez: ship it. Omar/Marcus please sync w/ Sean (SRE) so alerts don’t assume histogram availability.

Marcus Reed: will do. :white_check_mark:

source.text:
Hiro Tanaka: seeing a decode p95 regression when enabling the new INT8 profile on llama3.1-70b-instruct variant x1. baseline p95 decode ~38ms/token, INT8 profile jumps to ~52ms/token on A100-80GB. encode looks fine. anyone else seeing this?

Noah Patel: which profile id? and what runtime build?

Hiro Tanaka: profile ref `qpl:llama3.1-70b-x1:int8_w8a16:2025-02-19.3` (aka v3). runtime `runtime@b7f4c2a` from yesterday. route is pinned, no other changes.

Sofia Ivanova: is it only when continuous batching kicks in? (i.e. queue depth > 1)

Hiro Tanaka: happens even at qd=1. perf suite run `decode_only` shows it.

Omar El-Sayed: any chance we’re invalidating KV cache layout due to different pack/unpack? INT8 weights shouldn’t touch KV but we do some alignment changes for fused kernels.

Hiro Tanaka: grabbed nsight systems trace. looks like we’re not taking the fused attention kernel on decode; falling back to a slower path.

Noah Patel: can you paste the kernel names / top hotspots?

Hiro Tanaka: yep. from nsys summary (decode step, batch=1, seqlen=4096, new_tokens=64):
```
Time(%)  Total Time (ns)  Instances  Avg (ns)    Name
46.8     2.13e+09         64         3.33e+07    void at::native::scaled_dot_product_attention...
21.4     9.74e+08         128        7.61e+06    void cutlass::Kernel<...> gemm_f16...
12.2     5.55e+08         64         8.67e+06    void kv_cache::gather_scatter<<<...>>>
7.9      3.61e+08         64         5.64e+06    void layernorm<<<...>>>
```
Baseline run shows attention as `flash_fwd_splitkv` at ~22% instead of 46%.

Grace Kim: that suggests kernel selection is flipping. do we have a guard that disables flash attention when int8 weights enabled? (i vaguely remember a conservative check in pr-18479)

Hiro Tanaka: I checked logs with `RUNTIME_LOG_LEVEL=debug`, seeing:
`kernel_select: flash_attn=DISABLED reason=unsupported_combo(w8a16,int8_matmul)`
That seems… wrong? We shouldn’t tie attention kernel to weight GEMM mode.

Noah Patel: yeah, attention is independent. the guard might be too broad: “if quantized then disable flash” type thing.

Sofia Ivanova: is this only for v3 profile? what about v2?

Hiro Tanaka: v2 profile (`...:2025-02-12.1`) is fine. v3 is the one that regresses.

Jonas Weber: perf-canary run from last night flagged same thing on A100, didn’t repro on H100. (reacted :eyes:)

Omar El-Sayed: H100 path uses different attention backend; might hide it.

Grace Kim: v3 changed anything besides weights? e.g. activation dtype or smoothquant params? if it flipped act to fp16 vs bf16 it could trip a capability check.

Hiro Tanaka: profile says w8a16, activations fp16 (same as v2), but it also sets `preferred_attention_backend=auto` and `max_context=8192`. v2 had `max_context=4096`.

Noah Patel: ah. the “max_context” might be driving a conservative attention selection. maybe we disable flash if max_context > 4096 for that model family.

Sofia Ivanova: but your trace is seqlen=4096. even if max_context=8192, we should still allow flash at runtime when actual seqlen <= supported.

Mateo Alvarez: +1. selection should be based on actual request constraints, not profile max. unless the profile forces a different KV layout that breaks flash.

Omar El-Sayed: KV layout: for >4k we allocate a different paged cache config (larger page size) if `max_context` is higher. That might cause the flash kernel to be disabled because it can’t handle that page size.

Hiro Tanaka: interesting. I saw `kv_cache.page_size=256` in the v3 run, baseline/v2 is `page_size=128`.

Omar El-Sayed: there we go. our flash attention decode kernel only supports page_size=128 right now (and maybe 64). For 256 we fallback to sdpa.

Grace Kim: can we clamp page_size based on actual seqlen? or change heuristic: don’t bump page_size just because profile advertises max_context.

Noah Patel: we should also surface this as an explicit warning: “flash disabled due to kv_page_size=256” not “unsupported_combo(w8a16,int8_matmul)”. that error message is misleading.

Hiro Tanaka: agree. quick experiment: forced `KV_PAGE_SIZE=128` env var with v3 profile, decode p95 goes back to ~39ms/token.

Selene Huang: any risk changing page size affects correctness? (i.e. address calculations)

Omar El-Sayed: shouldn’t. page size is perf/memory tradeoff. correctness is same, just different indexing. But we need to ensure we don’t OOM for 8k contexts if we keep 128.

Jonas Weber: for A100 80GB, 70B @ 8k is already tight; bumping page size was meant to reduce metadata overhead. maybe the right fix is “page_size=128 if seqlen<=4096 else 256” (or pick dynamically at allocation).

Sofia Ivanova: + make it deterministic across replicas (same request -> same selection). but that’s fine if it’s purely a function of request seqlen + model.

Mateo Alvarez: action items:
1) fix kernel_select log reason (don’t blame int8)
2) adjust KV cache page sizing heuristic to be request-aware so we don’t disable flash for 4k runs
3) add perf-canary coverage specifically for “profile max_context > request seqlen” case

Hiro Tanaka: I can open a quick PR for #1/#2 unless Omar wants it.

Omar El-Sayed: I’ll take #2 (KV sizing). Hiro can do #1 (log reason + guard). Let’s coordinate to avoid conflicts.

Noah Patel: can someone paste the relevant config code path? I think it’s in `kv_cache/config.rs`.

Omar El-Sayed: it’s in `KvCacheConfig::from_profile()` currently:
```
if profile.max_context >= 8192 {
  page_size = 256;
}
```
We should move it to `KvCacheConfig::from_request(model, seqlen, profile)`.

Grace Kim: also update compatibility matrix docs? customers might ask why “INT8 made it slower” when it’s actually KV paging.

Hiro Tanaka: ok, I’ll add a metric tag too: `attention_backend=flash|sdpa` and `kv_page_size` in runtime span logs (if not already). helps with future triage.

Jonas Weber: please do. perf suite can then assert “flash still enabled” on the 4k decode benchmark.

Mateo Alvarez: keep me in the loop, this is close to beta. we should consider temporarily blocking v3 publication for A100 until fix lands (or mark as caveat).

Hiro Tanaka: ack. I’ll ping Arjun/Jonas separately about gating v3 for A100 with a caveat in the profile metadata.

Sofia Ivanova: thanks. also: can we add a regression test that enabling a profile with max_context=8192 doesn’t change decode kernel path for seqlen=4096? feels like exactly what we missed.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. I’ll add a small unit/integration test around KV config selection.

Hiro Tanaka: cool. posting numbers for record:
- baseline: 38.4ms/token p95 decode
- v3 default: 52.1ms/token p95 decode (flash disabled)
- v3 + KV_PAGE_SIZE=128: 39.2ms/token p95 decode

Mateo Alvarez: (reacted :thumbsup:)

source.content:
Noah Patel: we need to settle default watchdog thresholds for TP before we merge the abort/drain path. what are sane numbers by model size/workload? main worry is false positives -> draining healthy replicas.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. also we should be explicit if it’s a *stall* watchdog (no forward progress) vs *slow* watchdog (p99 latency). i think we want “no progress” only, but we’re using elapsed time as proxy.

Hiro Tanaka: agree. in kernel land we’ve seen collectives legitimately take longer during long-context prefill (esp with big batches), but decode step should be tight. recommendation: separate thresholds for (prefill step, decode iter, collective op).

Yuki Sato: quick data point from last week’s A100 80GB TP=8 runs (70B-ish):
- steady-state decode iter ~35–80ms
- worst spikes (not stuck) up to ~400–600ms when compaction + stream sync happens
- prefill for 16k context can be 2–6s depending on batch
so if decode watchdog is like 1s we’re probably safe, but 500ms might false-positive.

Grace Kim: +1, and cancelled streaming / client disconnect path can pause things too (we’re fixing but still). for safety i’d rather start higher and tighten.

Omar El-Sayed: another wrinkle: KV compaction / GC can block a stream for >1s under memory pressure. it’s not a deadlock but looks like “no progress”. we can tag stall reason once we have heartbeat + queue depth, but until then we’ll get ambiguous triggers.

Julian Moore: can we do two-stage? (1) “soft” watchdog logs + increments metric when timeout exceeded, (2) “hard” abort only when exceeded N times or exceeded much higher threshold. that reduces false-positive drains.

Noah Patel: yeah, we can implement soft->hard and keep hard behind flag.

Rafael Mendes: from SRE POV: please avoid anything that drains on first slow step. draining is disruptive + can cascade if you trip multiple replicas simultaneously. i’d prefer:
- default: emit structured error + mark replica unhealthy only if we’re *confident* it’s wedged
- ramp: logs-only in canary, then hard abort for a small % of Dedicated
also please document thresholds + override knobs.

Aisha Bello: and for alerting: we can page on “hard watchdog abort rate” not “soft exceeded”. soft can be dashboarded.

Tomáš Novák: runbook angle: if watchdog is too aggressive, oncall will just disable it. we should start conservative and have clear tuning guidance per model size + TP degree.

Marcus Reed: perf-canary can help here. i can add a scenario that intentionally stresses decode w/ bursty concurrency + long context, then we can look at the empirical p99 for decode iter and set watchdog = (p99 * 10?) or similar. but for now we need defaults to ship.

Hiro Tanaka: concrete proposal:
- decode_iter watchdog (hard): 10s (yes high). soft threshold: 2s.
- prefill_step watchdog (hard): 60s. soft: 15s.
- collective watchdog (hard): 20s. soft: 5s.
this is mostly “if it’s wedged, it’ll exceed these by a lot.”

Sofia Ivanova: 10s for decode feels *very* high. if you have a real deadlock you’re burning 10s per request before abort. but if abort triggers drain + retry maybe ok. tradeoff is customer-facing timeouts.

Noah Patel: note: request timeouts at API are often 30–60s for streaming, but customers notice p99 inflation. if we wait 10s to decide “stall”, we still blow SLO.

Yuki Sato: what about dynamic threshold based on observed median? something like:
`decode_watchdog = max(2s, 20x EMA(decode_iter_ms))`
then it scales w/ smaller models too.

Julian Moore: dynamic is nice but risky for v1. if EMA is wrong early after deploy, might be too low/high. also need per-model/per-route tracking.

Omar El-Sayed: plus under true stall EMA never updates, so you still need an absolute ceiling.

Rafael Mendes: +1 keep it simple for now. dynamic can come later once metrics stable.

Sofia Ivanova: can we compromise: decode hard = 5s, soft = 1.5s? prefill hard = 45s soft = 10s? collective hard = 15s soft = 3s?

Grace Kim: i’m ok with that if hard abort is *disabled by default* and we start with soft-only. hard enable in canary after a week of seeing false positives.

Noah Patel: that matches the progressive rollout plan. we’ll do:
1) runtime.tp_watchdog.enabled = false (default)
2) runtime.tp_watchdog.mode = "observe" (soft-only) in perf-canary + one Dedicated canary cluster
3) flip to "enforce" for 5% Dedicated canary after we validate false positive rate

Connor O'Brien: for release planning (1.19), can we land the code with defaults + flag off, then enable observe during canary window? we need the config knobs in place for the rollout checklist.

Marissa Cole: yes. go/no-go for enabling enforce should require: (a) observe has <0.1% soft trips per 10k req on the canary workloads, (b) no correlated p99 regression, (c) SRE signoff.

Aisha Bello: +1 and we should tag metrics by model_family + tp_degree + phase (prefill/decode) so we can see where it’s noisy.

Noah Patel: ok, proposed config keys (naming TBD but close):
```yaml
runtime:
  tp_watchdog:
    enabled: false
    mode: observe   # observe|enforce
    decode_iter_soft_timeout_ms: 1500
    decode_iter_hard_timeout_ms: 5000
    prefill_soft_timeout_ms: 10000
    prefill_hard_timeout_ms: 45000
    collective_soft_timeout_ms: 3000
    collective_hard_timeout_ms: 15000
    consecutive_hard_timeouts_to_drain: 1
```
if mode=observe, we never drain, just log/metrics.

Tomáš Novák: can we set consecutive_hard_timeouts_to_drain=2? (even in enforce) just to be safe? one-off hiccup shouldn’t drain.

Hiro Tanaka: i worry that if it’s a true deadlock, you’ll just waste time twice. but ok if hard is 5s; 2x5s still better than hanging indefinitely.

Rafael Mendes: i strongly prefer 2 for initial enforce rollout. reduces blast radius if thresholds are wrong.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. once we have better stall reason classification we can lower it.

Noah Patel: ok: consecutive_hard_timeouts_to_drain=2 for initial. also we’ll include a “progress heartbeat” check; if any rank heartbeat updates, we can suppress drain and just emit soft trip (requires ENG-4823).

Omar El-Sayed: careful: heartbeat update could happen even in partial progress while collectives mismatched. but as a guardrail it’s fine.

Marcus Reed: i’ll add perf-canary thresholds + capture: decode_iter_ms distribution + watchdog_trip_count. we can revisit numbers after 2–3 days of canary data.

Connor O'Brien: please drop these defaults into the release ticket notes + rollout checklist. also need a one-liner for support: “watchdog observe-only, no behavior change” until we flip.

Marissa Cole: and add an explicit rollback: if enforce causes >0.5% drained replicas/day or customer-visible error spike, disable enforce immediately.

Noah Patel: done. i’ll post an update in the ADR thread once the numbers are in the PR. :thumbsup:

Aisha Bello: :thumbsup: once merged ping me and Sergio so we can wire the dashboards to the soft vs hard trip metrics.

Rafael Mendes: thanks. when you enable observe on canary, please give SRE 24h heads up so we can monitor + ensure no surprise paging.

Noah Patel: will do.

Julian Moore: Need to lock naming/labels for TP heartbeat + stall reason metrics today so PR can go up. I’m proposing Prometheus-style names (snake_case) + a small set of labels.

Julian Moore: Current draft:
- `runtime_tp_rank_heartbeat_last_seen_seconds` (gauge, unix ts)
- `runtime_tp_rank_heartbeat_gap_seconds` (gauge, derived in-process)
- `runtime_tp_stall_events_total{reason=...}` (counter)
- `runtime_tp_inflight_collectives{collective=...}` (gauge)

Omar El-Sayed: +1 on `last_seen_seconds`. I’d avoid emitting `gap_seconds` from every rank if we can compute in queries (now() - last_seen). But I get the “alertable” point.

Noah Patel: Agree on keeping it minimal. The key is: can SRE page on something deterministic (rank heartbeat missing) AND can we triage (stall reason code).

Aisha Bello: From SRE side: please keep names stable + alertable. We can do alerting off `time() - last_seen` in PromQL; don’t need a separate gap gauge. Also cardinality: rank label is already high-ish; don’t add request_id / prompt_id / seq_len bucket labels.

Aisha Bello: For alerting, I’d want:
- heartbeat missing per replica (not per request)
- stall reason counts per cluster/model to see what’s dominating

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also if `last_seen` is unix ts, we can use `max_over_time` / `time()` to compute gaps.

Julian Moore: OK, so drop `heartbeat_gap_seconds`. Keep only `last_seen`.

Allison Grant: Re: naming conventions: we’ve been using `runtime_` prefix in engine metrics, and units in the name (`_seconds`, `_total`). So `runtime_tp_rank_heartbeat_last_seen_seconds` is fine.

Sergio Costa: For dashboards/alerts, it’s easier if the “thing you page on” is a single metric name. But PromQL can handle it.

Julian Moore: Proposed labels for heartbeat:
`runtime_tp_rank_heartbeat_last_seen_seconds{cluster, region, deployment_mode, model, model_revision, replica_id, tp_degree, tp_rank}`

Caleb Johnson: `model_revision` is potentially high cardinality if it includes git SHA or full semver+build. Can we standardize it to the runtime “model version” string (the pinned serving version)?

Omar El-Sayed: In runtime we have `model_id` and `model_version` already (e.g., `llama-70b`, `2025-02-11.1`). We should reuse that.

Aisha Bello: Please do NOT include `replica_id` if it’s a random UUID that churns constantly. If it’s stable per pod name, that’s OK, but we should be careful.

Rafael Mendes: For Dedicated, replica identifiers churn during autoscaling; still useful for debugging but can hurt aggregate dashboards.

Noah Patel: Can we split: always include `replica` label (stable-ish) but keep `pod_uid` / truly unique ids out?

Julian Moore: In our infra, we have `replica` as `<service>-<shard>-<ordinal>` (k8s statefulset-ish), and `replica_uid` is the UUID. I can use `replica` only.

Aisha Bello: That works. And please include `tp_rank` as a small int, `tp_degree` as small int. Good.

Julian Moore: Cool. Revised heartbeat metric:
- `runtime_tp_rank_heartbeat_last_seen_seconds{cluster, region, deployment_mode, model_id, model_version, replica, tp_degree, tp_rank}`

Allison Grant: +1. Also use `deployment_mode` values: `hosted|dedicated|private` (we’ve standardized this elsewhere).

Julian Moore: Now stall reason codes. Draft metric:
- `runtime_tp_stall_events_total{reason, stage}`
Where stage is `prefill|decode|unknown`.

Omar El-Sayed: Can we add `component`? e.g. `scheduler|comms|memory|unknown` so we can roll up quickly.

Aisha Bello: Having both `component` and `reason` is good if reason list is stable/small. Please keep it to an enum; don’t dump raw error strings.

Noah Patel: Yes, `reason` should be a code, not freeform.

Julian Moore: Proposed reasons (enum):
- `scheduler_collective_starvation`
- `nccl_collective_timeout`
- `cuda_stream_wait_timeout`
- `rank_divergence_detected`
- `kv_cache_compaction_wait`
- `oom_recovery_loop`
- `request_cancelled_collective_wait`
- `unknown`

Rafael Mendes: `nccl_collective_timeout` vs `nccl_collective_stall`? We might not always “timeout” (sometimes we detect via no-progress heartbeat). I’d prefer `nccl_collective_stall`.

Aisha Bello: +1. “timeout” implies watchdog fired; stall is broader.

Omar El-Sayed: Also `rank_divergence_detected` is good, but let’s ensure it’s only emitted when we have positive signal (e.g. mismatch in collective seqno). Otherwise it’ll become “unknown but we guessed divergence”.

Julian Moore: ok, rename to `rank_collective_seq_mismatch` (only when we see mismatch).

Allison Grant: For metric naming: if it’s “stall events”, counter is fine. If we want duration distribution later, add histogram separately.

Julian Moore: Stall metric final proposal:
- `runtime_tp_stall_events_total{cluster, region, deployment_mode, model_id, model_version, tp_degree, reason, component, stage}`
No replica label (to keep cardinality sane). For per-replica debugging we’ll rely on logs + trace spans.

Sergio Costa: Works for dashboards. For per-replica panels we can still do heartbeat-based views using `replica` label.

Caleb Johnson: For reason/component/stage label sets, keep values lowercase snake_case.

Aisha Bello: Alerting guidance:
- Page when `time() - runtime_tp_rank_heartbeat_last_seen_seconds` > 30s for any `replica` AND persists 2-3m (avoid flaps during deploy).
- Ticket-only alert for elevated `runtime_tp_stall_events_total` rate by reason (e.g. >N/min) correlated with request timeouts.

Rafael Mendes: Agree. Also add runbook link in alert annotations (Tomáš is working on the TP stall triage playbook).

Julian Moore: Great. I’ll bake the alertability notes into the PR description.

Noah Patel: One more: for the heartbeat metric, do we emit from each rank process? We need all ranks to report separately.

Julian Moore: Yes, one sample per rank, with `tp_rank` label. Emitted on a 2s cadence from the TP engine loop.

Omar El-Sayed: Make sure heartbeat updates during both prefill and decode; we don’t want “silent” periods during long prefill kernels.

Julian Moore: Yup, heartbeat will be in a lightweight host-side tick + also on collective boundaries.

Allison Grant: Please add a short comment in code that `last_seen_seconds` is wall clock (not monotonic) so future folks don’t try to diff it for latency.

Julian Moore: Done. Summary of decisions:
1) Heartbeat metric: `runtime_tp_rank_heartbeat_last_seen_seconds{cluster, region, deployment_mode, model_id, model_version, replica, tp_degree, tp_rank}`
2) Stall metric: `runtime_tp_stall_events_total{cluster, region, deployment_mode, model_id, model_version, tp_degree, reason, component, stage}` (no replica)
3) Use `nccl_collective_stall` (not timeout) and `rank_collective_seq_mismatch` for divergence
4) No request-level labels. Use logs/traces for deep dives.

Aisha Bello: Looks good from SRE. Please @me on the PR; I’ll sanity check label cardinality once it’s in code.

Rafael Mendes: +1. Also include `deployment_mode` values exactly as Allison said.

Julian Moore: Will do. Shipping the metric naming updates now.

source.text:
Omar El-Sayed: Need to settle the hit/miss definitions today so Samir can finish counters + Nadia can lock schema. Main confusion: KV cache hit vs prefix cache hit, and what “partial reuse” means esp multi-turn chat.

Samir Qureshi: +1. Implementation wise I can emit (a) tokens reused (skipped compute) and (b) tokens eligible. But people keep asking for “request hit rate” which gets weird w partial.

Noah Patel: Let’s be super explicit about numerator/denominator and *stage*. KV cache is about reuse across turns within a session (or same route) at decode-time, but prefix cache is prefill-time reuse of prompt prefix. If we mix the two it’ll be garbage.

Sofia Ivanova: Also continuous batching complicates attribution. Prefill for multiple reqs in one batch, we need per-request counts. We can’t have “hit” mean “batch-level cache was warm”.

Omar El-Sayed: Proposal:
- Prefix cache metrics computed at prefill stage.
- KV cache metrics computed during decode stage.
But question: for prefill, is “hit” measured as prompt tokens reused (prefix reuse depth) / prompt tokens requested? Or only eligible tokens (excluding system prompt? tool schema? etc.)

Nadia Rahman: From schema/cardinality side: want stable semantic: “eligible_prefill_tokens” is whatever runtime attempted to prefill (after canonicalization / truncation). Don’t try to carve out “system vs user” in metrics.

Allison Grant: For dashboards, SRE wants to see “why did GPU cost spike”: prefill compute went up because prefix reuse went down OR decode got more expensive because KV reuse went down. So split is good.

Samir Qureshi: KV cache doesn’t really “reuse tokens” in decode; it reuses KV tensors for prior context tokens so each new token decode avoids recomputing attention over previous tokens. So the natural unit is “context tokens served from cache” per decode step.

Noah Patel: Right: in decode step t, context length = L. If KV cache has entries for L tokens, it’s a hit. If not, we pay a prefill-like recompute / rebuild KV.

Omar El-Sayed: This is where partial comes in: you can have KV for first K tokens but missing tail due to eviction/version mismatch. Do we count partial as hit?

Sofia Ivanova: We should. Otherwise every eviction makes hit-rate binary and noisy. But we need two metrics: “full hit” vs “reuse fraction”.

Marcus Reed: Perf view: I care about “wasted prefill” too: tokens we prefilled that didn’t contribute to subsequent decode reuse (due to miss/evict). Partial reuse should correlate with wasted prefill.

Allison Grant: For alerting, a single scalar is still useful. But it has to be stable. We can alert on drop in “reused_prefill_tokens / total_prefill_tokens” and separately on “kv_reuse_fraction”.

Nadia Rahman: Naming from standards doc: I’d like token-based ratios, not request-based. Request-based is workload-shape dependent and hard to compare across prompt buckets.

Samir Qureshi: So for prefix cache:
- total_prefill_tokens = prompt tokens we would have computed in prefill
- prefix_reused_tokens = tokens loaded from prefix cache (i.e., skip compute)
- prefix_reuse_ratio = prefix_reused_tokens / total_prefill_tokens
And optional histogram on reuse depth.

Noah Patel: Careful: “total_prefill_tokens” includes tokens that are *not cacheable*? Like if caching disabled for route.

Omar El-Sayed: Good point. Denominator should be “cache_eligible_prefill_tokens”. Then if caching disabled, we don’t tank hit-rate, we emit misses by reason.

Nadia Rahman: Yes. Emit “eligible” and then “miss_reason” on the misses. That’s more actionable.

Allison Grant: +1 please. Otherwise dashboard says 0% hit-rate and oncall spends 2h only to find someone flipped policy off.

Samir Qureshi: OK so prefix cache:
- eligible_prefill_tokens
- reused_prefill_tokens
- missed_prefill_tokens = eligible - reused
and miss_reason breakdown: policy_disabled, ttl_expired, capacity, hash_mismatch, version_mismatch (do we need all?)

Omar El-Sayed: On miss taxonomy: keep it small/stable. I’m fine with those 5.

Sofia Ivanova: Where does “evicted” land? That’s capacity, right? We can have eviction metrics separately.

Noah Patel: For KV cache, define:
- kv_context_tokens_needed: how many context tokens decode expects to have cached (post any truncation)
- kv_context_tokens_present: tokens actually present in KV cache
- kv_reuse_ratio = present / needed
Plus a counter for “kv_miss_event” when present < needed, with miss_reason.

Samir Qureshi: Implementation detail: “needed” can change within a request because of sliding window / max_seq? but our runtime uses fixed context window per request. I can compute per decode step, but that’s heavy.

Marcus Reed: Please don’t do per-step metrics in prod, overhead will be awful. Aggregate per request: evaluate at first decode step only.

Omar El-Sayed: Agree: define KV reuse at boundary between prefill->decode: before first decode token, how many KV entries are already there vs needed for context. That’s the moment that matters for latency spikes.

Noah Patel: This also handles multi-turn chat: turn N starts with some existing cache. We measure reuse at turn start.

Allison Grant: How do we treat streaming where client cancels mid-generation? Does that affect denominators?

Samir Qureshi: For prefix cache, prefill already happened, so it’s counted. For KV reuse, we measure before first decode, so cancellation after doesn’t matter. That’s fine.

Sofia Ivanova: Retries though… gateway can retry streaming on transient. We can double count unless we de-dupe by request_id.

Omar El-Sayed: We are *not* logging raw request_id as a label. But runtime can guard by “only emit once per logical request” using internal attempt counter.

Nadia Rahman: +1. No high-card labels. If you need debugging, annotate a trace span not metric label.

Allison Grant: Exactly. Metrics: low-card. Traces: per-request.

Marcus Reed: So what is “hit-rate” in English in ADR? People will ask.

Noah Patel: I’d write:
- Prefix cache hit-rate: fraction of cache-eligible prefill tokens served from prefix cache.
- KV cache hit-rate (reuse effectiveness): fraction of context tokens already present in KV cache at the start of decoding for a request/turn.
And define “partial hit” explicitly as ratio in [0,1].

Samir Qureshi: Do we still need a boolean “full_hit” metric?

Allison Grant: For dashboards, nice to have “full hit %” as a separate panel. But don’t call it “hit-rate”.

Nadia Rahman: If we do it, make it a counter: full_hit_requests_total and eligible_requests_total, ratio derived in query. But careful with workload mix changes.

Omar El-Sayed: Let’s keep request-level as optional. Primary is token-based. Request-based can be added later if SRE insists.

Sofia Ivanova: Re: multi-turn attribution: if a chat has 10 turns, each turn emits its own prefill metrics and kv reuse metrics. That’s fine.

Noah Patel: One nuance: prefix cache could be “warm” because last turn shares a lot of prefix. But if prompts include timestamps / random IDs, hash_mismatch will dominate. That’s the actionable bit.

Allison Grant: That’s why miss_reason needs hash_mismatch. We’ll point customers at canonicalization.

Marcus Reed: Also “version_mismatch”: model rollout invalidates cached tensors. We need this to catch SUP-3912 style regressions.

Nadia Rahman: OK then.
Action items:
- Omar: update ADR text w token-based definitions + partial semantics + stage boundaries.
- Samir: implement eligible/reused counters and KV reuse ratio as per-turn at first decode.
- Sofia: sanity check in continuous batching path for per-request attribution.
- Allison: dashboard definitions: separate panels for prefix vs KV, avoid single blended “cache hit-rate”.

Mateo Alvarez: +1 to this direction. Keep it simple and stable. Token-based primary metric, request-based optional. Also please add a small “how to interpret” blurb in the internal readme so people stop equating KV and prefix.

Omar El-Sayed: Cool. I’ll post ADR draft in #architecture for final review by EOD. :thumbsup:

Samir Qureshi: I’ll adjust the enum to those 5 miss reasons and wire the denominator as eligible tokens. Will ping if I hit ambiguity around ttl_expired vs capacity.

Allison Grant: When you have the metric names, drop them here so I can start panel queries.

Nadia Rahman: Please use the naming from standards: `cache_prefix_*` and `cache_kv_*` prefixes (and no “hit_rate” suffix for the raw counters).

Omar El-Sayed: Ack. Will align names with Nadia’s doc once I paste the final list.

Sofia Ivanova: Also flag: scheduler can cause locality changes; not a miss reason but shows up as hit drop. Might want a tag for region/pool (low-card) so we can see it.

Nadia Rahman: Region is allowed. Pool is tricky (cardinality). Let’s stick to deployment_mode + region + model + route_id allowlist.

Allison Grant: Works for SRE. Pool is too granular anyway.

Omar El-Sayed: Closing loop: definitions above are the ones we’ll implement unless objections in the next hour.

Allison Grant: For dashboards, nice to have “full hit %” as a separate panel. But don’t call it “hit-rate”.

Nadia Rahman: If we do it, make it a counter: full_hit_requests_total and eligible_requests_total, ratio derived in query. But careful with workload mix changes.

Omar El-Sayed: Let’s keep request-level as optional. Primary is token-based. Request-based can be added later if SRE insists.

Sofia Ivanova: Re: multi-turn attribution: if a chat has 10 turns, each turn emits its own prefill metrics and kv reuse metrics. That’s fine.

Noah Patel: One nuance: prefix cache could be “warm” because last turn shares a lot of prefix. But if prompts include timestamps / random IDs, hash_mismatch will dominate. That’s the actionable bit.

Allison Grant: That’s why miss_reason needs hash_mismatch. We’ll point customers at canonicalization.

Marcus Reed: Also “version_mismatch”: model rollout invalidates cached tensors. We need this to catch SUP-3912 style regressions.

Nadia Rahman: OK then.

- Omar: update ADR text w token-based definitions + partial semantics + stage boundaries.
- Samir: implement eligible/reused counters and KV reuse ratio as per-turn at first decode.
- Sofia: sanity check in continuous batching path for per-request attribution.
- Allison: dashboard definitions: separate panels for prefix vs KV, avoid single blended “cache hit-rate”.

Mateo Alvarez: +1 to this direction. Keep it simple and stable. Token-based primary metric, request-based optional. Also please add a small “how to interpret” blurb in the internal readme so people stop equating KV and prefix.

Omar El-Sayed: Cool. I’ll post ADR draft in #architecture for final review by EOD. :thumbsup:

Samir Qureshi: I’ll adjust the enum to those 5 miss reasons and wire the denominator as eligible tokens. Will ping if I hit ambiguity around ttl_expired vs capacity.

Allison Grant: When you have the metric names, drop them here so I can start panel queries.

Nadia Rahman: Please use the naming from standards: `cache_prefix_*` and `cache_kv_*` prefixes (and no “hit_rate” suffix for the raw counters).

Omar El-Sayed: Ack. Will align names with Nadia’s doc once I paste the final list.

Sofia Ivanova: Also flag: scheduler can cause locality changes; not a miss reason but shows up as hit drop. Might want a tag for region/pool (low-card) so we can see it.

Nadia Rahman: Region is allowed. Pool is tricky (cardinality). Let’s stick to deployment_mode + region + model + route_id allowlist.

Allison Grant: Works for SRE. Pool is too granular anyway.

Omar El-Sayed: Need to settle the hit/miss definitions today so Samir can finish counters + Nadia can lock schema. Main confusion: KV cache hit vs prefix cache hit, and what “partial reuse” means esp multi-turn chat.

Samir Qureshi: +1. Implementation wise I can emit (a) tokens reused (skipped compute) and (b) tokens eligible. But people keep asking for “request hit rate” which gets weird w partial.

Noah Patel: Let’s be super explicit about numerator/denominator and *stage*. KV cache is about reuse across turns within a session (or same route) at decode-time, but prefix cache is prefill-time reuse of prompt prefix. If we mix the two it’ll be garbage.

Sofia Ivanova: Also continuous batching complicates attribution. Prefill for multiple reqs in one batch, we need per-request counts. We can’t have “hit” mean “batch-level cache was warm”.

Omar El-Sayed: Proposal:
- Prefix cache metrics computed at prefill stage.
- KV cache metrics computed during decode stage.
But question: for prefill, is “hit” measured as prompt tokens reused (prefix reuse depth) / prompt tokens requested? Or only eligible tokens (excluding system prompt? tool schema? etc.)

Nadia Rahman: From schema/cardinality side: want stable semantic: “eligible_prefill_tokens” is whatever runtime attempted to prefill (after canonicalization / truncation). Don’t try to carve out “system vs user” in metrics.

Allison Grant: For dashboards, SRE wants to see “why did GPU cost spike”: prefill compute went up because prefix reuse went down OR decode got more expensive because KV reuse went down. So split is good.

Samir Qureshi: KV cache doesn’t really “reuse tokens” in decode; it reuses KV tensors for prior context tokens so each new token decode avoids recomputing attention over previous tokens. So the natural unit is “context tokens served from cache” per decode step.

Noah Patel: Right: in decode step t, context length = L. If KV cache has entries for L tokens, it’s a hit. If not, we pay a prefill-like recompute / rebuild KV.

Omar El-Sayed: This is where partial comes in: you can have KV for first K tokens but missing tail due to eviction/version mismatch. Do we count partial as hit?

Sofia Ivanova: We should. Otherwise every eviction makes hit-rate binary and noisy. But we need two metrics: “full hit” vs “reuse fraction”.

Marcus Reed: Perf view: I care about “wasted prefill” too: tokens we prefilled that didn’t contribute to subsequent decode reuse (due to miss/evict). Partial reuse should correlate with wasted prefill.

Allison Grant: For alerting, a single scalar is still useful. But it has to be stable. We can alert on drop in “reused_prefill_tokens / total_prefill_tokens” and separately on “kv_reuse_fraction”.

Nadia Rahman: Naming from standards doc: I’d like token-based ratios, not request-based. Request-based is workload-shape dependent and hard to compare across prompt buckets.

Samir Qureshi: So for prefix cache:
- total_prefill_tokens = prompt tokens we would have computed in prefill
- prefix_reused_tokens = tokens loaded from prefix cache (i.e., skip compute)
- prefix_reuse_ratio = prefix_reused_tokens / total_prefill_tokens
And optional histogram on reuse depth.

Noah Patel: Careful: “total_prefill_tokens” includes tokens that are *not cacheable*? Like if caching disabled for route.

Omar El-Sayed: Good point. Denominator should be “cache_eligible_prefill_tokens”. Then if caching disabled, we don’t tank hit-rate, we emit misses by reason.

Nadia Rahman: Yes. Emit “eligible” and then “miss_reason” on the misses. That’s more actionable.

Allison Grant: +1 please. Otherwise dashboard says 0% hit-rate and oncall spends 2h only to find someone flipped policy off.

Samir Qureshi: OK so prefix cache:
- eligible_prefill_tokens
- reused_prefill_tokens
- missed_prefill_tokens = eligible - reused
and miss_reason breakdown: policy_disabled, ttl_expired, capacity, hash_mismatch, version_mismatch (do we need all?)

Omar El-Sayed: On miss taxonomy: keep it small/stable. I’m fine with those 5.

Sofia Ivanova: Where does “evicted” land? That’s capacity, right? We can have eviction metrics separately.

Noah Patel: For KV cache, define:
- kv_context_tokens_needed: how many context tokens decode expects to have cached (post any truncation)
- kv_context_tokens_present: tokens actually present in KV cache
- kv_reuse_ratio = present / needed
Plus a counter for “kv_miss_event” when present < needed, with miss_reason.

Samir Qureshi: Implementation detail: “needed” can change within a request because of sliding window / max_seq? but our runtime uses fixed context window per request. I can compute per decode step, but that’s heavy.

Marcus Reed: Please don’t do per-step metrics in prod, overhead will be awful. Aggregate per request: evaluate at first decode step only.

Omar El-Sayed: Agree: define KV reuse at boundary between prefill->decode: before first decode token, how many KV entries are already there vs needed for context. That’s the moment that matters for latency spikes.

Noah Patel: This also handles multi-turn chat: turn N starts with some existing cache. We measure reuse at turn start.

Allison Grant: How do we treat streaming where client cancels mid-generation? Does that affect denominators?

Samir Qureshi: For prefix cache, prefill already happened, so it’s counted. For KV reuse, we measure before first decode, so cancellation after doesn’t matter. That’s fine.

Sofia Ivanova: Retries though… gateway can retry streaming on transient. We can double count unless we de-dupe by request_id.

Omar El-Sayed: We are *not* logging raw request_id as a label. But runtime can guard by “only emit once per logical request” using internal attempt counter.

Nadia Rahman: +1. No high-card labels. If you need debugging, annotate a trace span not metric label.

Allison Grant: Exactly. Metrics: low-card. Traces: per-request.

Marcus Reed: So what is “hit-rate” in English in ADR? People will ask.

Noah Patel: I’d write:
- Prefix cache hit-rate: fraction of cache-eligible prefill tokens served from prefix cache.
- KV cache hit-rate (reuse effectiveness): fraction of context tokens already present in KV cache at the start of decoding for a request/turn.

source.content:
Marcus Lin: quick gut-check for the hosted data residency work — any chance the *runtime* layer logs/traces could accidentally move customer content cross-region?

Marcus Lin: specifically thinking:
- runtime logs (engine / model server)
- OTel traces/span attrs
- crash dumps / “debug mode” outputs

Marcus Lin: asking because enforcement boundary in ADR-021 is “customer content stays in allowed region(s)” and I don’t want a surprise where EU traffic still exports traces to a US collector.

Mina Haddad: +1. Routing side is being pinned, but if runtime exports traces to global endpoint we’re leaking metadata at minimum.

Ravi Menon: what’s the current setup for inference spans? are we attaching prompt/response anywhere (even truncated) for debugging?

Allison Grant: current standard is OTel spans from gateway + router, and runtime emits a few spans (decode loop timing, batch size, cache hit) via our `runtime-telemetry` module.

Allison Grant: *policy* is “no raw prompt/response in spans”. But I’m not 100% sure nobody added a “helpful” attr somewhere.

Allison Grant: also yes — exporters are currently configured per cluster, but the collector endpoint for some envs is still `otel-collector.prod.us-east-1.redwood.internal` (historical).

Sean Gallagher: SRE view: logs go to region-local Fluent Bit -> region-local OpenSearch. BUT traces: a bunch of clusters still forward to the shared collector in us-east (then to Honeycomb). So even if attrs are clean, the trace data itself is leaving region.

Jordan Lee: residency definition we’ve been using: customer content includes prompts, responses, fine-tunes, uploaded files, tool call args *if customer-provided*, etc. Metadata is allowed cross-region only if explicitly listed (request_id, org_id, model id, timings). Trace payloads would need to be scrubbed AND exporters should be region-scoped.

Nikhil Sharma: runtime side: engine logs are mostly perf + CUDA warnings. BUT we do have a couple places where we log “bad request payload” when JSON parsing fails upstream and the raw body gets included in the exception message.

Nikhil Sharma: I *think* that’s in gateway, not runtime, but we should confirm. In runtime, the only thing that could accidentally include text is when tokenizer throws and we log the offending segment (rare). Need to search.

Elena Popov: router adds `x-redwood-trace-context` and we propagate to runtime. I don’t think we propagate any customer content fields, but spans can include request route tags.

Logan Wright: from audit/logging standpoint: we should treat traces as “logs” for residency constraints. If trace export crosses region, it’s a control gap even if we only export hashed IDs.

Ethan Park: control plane side doesn’t touch runtime, but we can add a config guardrail: residency-enabled orgs/projects -> force `otel_export=region_local_only` (or disable runtime span export) until we finish migrating collectors.

Allison Grant: action proposal:
1) runtime code audit: ensure no prompt/response/tool args in logs or span attrs.
2) config change: per-region OTel collector endpoints, no cross-region forwarding.
3) fallback: for residency-enforced tenants, disable runtime span export entirely (only emit local metrics) until we’re confident.

Ravi Menon: +1. For (1) we should also verify “debug” flags in runtime aren’t enabled in prod (some libs print input tokens).

Sean Gallagher: I can own (2). We can stand up EU collector in `eu-central-1` and point EU clusters at it. Same for `eu-west-1`. For US, keep us-east. Need to check if Honeycomb dataset is region-specific or still centralized.

Allison Grant: Honeycomb is still centralized for now. But we can keep collector region-local and then apply a “residency scrubber” processor before exporting.

Marcus Lin: careful: even if scrubbed, “exporting to Honeycomb US” might still violate customer expectation depending on how we phrase guarantees. We should align with Naomi/Olga on what’s permitted cross-region.

Jordan Lee: ADR-021 draft language: cross-region is allowed for “operational metadata excluding customer content,” but it’s conservative. For enterprise EU-only, they’re expecting *everything* stays EU unless explicitly stated.

Mina Haddad: for the initial release, can we mandate: if region pinning is enabled for an org, traces must remain in-region (collector + storage) OR be disabled? That’s easier to reason about.

Sean Gallagher: doable. We can disable trace export via env var at the collector or daemonset per namespace. Something like setting `OTEL_TRACES_EXPORTER=none` for runtime pods in EU until storage is EU.

Allison Grant: yep, and still emit metrics via Prometheus (in-region).

Nikhil Sharma: for (1) I’ll do a grep pass in runtime repos today:
- search for `prompt`, `completion`, `input_text`, `messages`, `tool_args`
- check tokenizer error handling
- check “request dump” in exception paths

Nikhil Sharma: and I’ll add a unit test that ensures we never log the raw request payload on JSON errors (sanitize to length + hash).

Logan Wright: I can add a “residency_redaction” filter to our structured logging schema and make it default for runtime/logger. But that’s slightly bigger.

Marcus Lin: minimal mitigations we need for GA:
- ensure runtime logs never include raw customer content
- ensure runtime traces don’t leave region for pinned tenants

Marcus Lin: owners?

Sean Gallagher: I’ll own trace pipeline changes + rollout plan (EU first). Will coordinate w/ Rafael.

Allison Grant: I’ll own audit of span attributes + update telemetry guidelines (what attrs allowed). Also will add a CI check in `runtime-telemetry` to block string fields named like `prompt`/`messages` from being added.

Nikhil Sharma: I’ll own runtime log audit + patch any offending log lines. Will open a ticket with findings.

Jordan Lee: I’ll sync with Naomi + Olga on the “are scrubbed traces allowed cross-region” question and update the boundary doc accordingly.

Mina Haddad: once we have the decision, routing will enforce a header/flag to runtime so it can disable traces per request/tenant if needed.

Elena Popov: router can attach `residency_enforced=true` in context headers; runtime can read and drop tracing.

Marcus Lin: perfect. please post findings here + links to tickets/PRs.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: will post a plan later today. Expect: EU clusters switch to EU collector, and for pinned tenants we’ll set traces exporter off by default until we have EU storage.

Allison Grant: also adding a dashboard panel: “runtime spans exported by region” so we can prove no EU -> US export when pinning enabled.

Nikhil Sharma: starting code scan now. will report back with any risky callsites.

Marcus Lin: thanks all. this was exactly the gap I was worried about.

source.messages:
Noah Patel: Heads up from last night’s rollback drill: we saw a noticeable p95/p99 spike right after rollback when we invalidate caches. Concern: rollback is “fix the regression” but we might temporarily make latency worse.

Noah Patel: Specifically: rollback flips optimization config version, runtime applies + does cache invalidation (prefix/KV + some routing caches). Workers drop hot prefixes and we get a burst of cold prefill.

Omar El-Sayed: yep, that matches what I’ve been seeing in local profiling. invalidation is effectively forcing prefill on first N requests. if rollback happens under load, we get queueing + p99 balloons.

Sofia Ivanova: do we have numbers from the gameday? how bad was the spike + how long did it last

Noah Patel: from the drill: steady-state p95 ~240ms, during rollback window p95 ~410ms for ~2-3 mins. p99 went from ~900ms to ~2.6s at peak, then recovered. errors didn’t move much.

Sean Gallagher: from SRE POV this is a problem because the rollback itself can trip the latency gate + paging. We need to annotate dashboards as “rollback in progress” but also try to keep the spike bounded.

Allison Grant: +1. also note: our latency SLO gate eval is currently blind to “expected disturbance” windows unless we explicitly mark the rollout controller state.

Omar El-Sayed: what’s being invalidated today on rollback?

Noah Patel: currently:
- `PrefixCache` entries keyed by (model, tenant, normalized_prompt_prefix)
- `KvCacheManager` reuse pool per worker
- “route hint” cache (model variant selection / kernel profile)

Noah Patel: reason was correctness: new config can change batching/caching knobs, and quant profiles can change tokenization-ish behavior / logits quality, so we wanted to avoid mixed assumptions.

Sofia Ivanova: for batching heuristics rollback, do we actually need to drop prefix cache? feels like it’s safe? prefixes are content-based, not dependent on batching

Omar El-Sayed: prefix cache is “safe” if it’s just storing KV for exact same prefix and same model weights. but if quant profile changes, the KV state differs (weights different). using KV from v_new with v_old weights would be wrong.

Noah Patel: right. but in most rollbacks we’re rolling back batching/caching knobs without changing weights/quant. the question: can we do conditional invalidation based on what fields changed.

Benji Okafor: (from control plane side) diff engine already classifies “risky fields”. we could add a runtime hint like `requires_cache_flush: true/false` derived from semantic validation. would that help?

Noah Patel: yes, that’s one mitigation: only flush what you must.

Allison Grant: can we enumerate which changes require which flush? that also helps observability (“rollback type: warm cache safe”)

Omar El-Sayed: proposal:
1) quant_profile_id change => must flush PrefixCache + KV reuse pool (and maybe force a worker restart if we can’t guarantee no stale)
2) cache_enabled toggle or cache_max_bytes changes => do NOT flush immediately; instead, let entries expire naturally + stop admitting new entries if disabled
3) batching params (max_batch_tokens, target_latency_ms, etc) => no cache flush needed

Sofia Ivanova: +1 on (3). batching rollback should be mostly scheduler-side; cache should be independent.

Sean Gallagher: what about “route hint cache”? if rollback changes kernel selection thresholds, could stale hints cause a bad kernel pick?

Noah Patel: route hint cache is mostly a perf optimization. stale hints are annoying but not correctness-breaking; we can keep them + revalidate on hit. we should change that code path.

Noah Patel: today it’s “trust cache”. we can make it “trust but verify”: if cached hint points to kernel/profile not compatible with current config, recompute.

Omar El-Sayed: that would avoid a mass invalidate too.

perf-canary-bot: perf-canary (rollback-drill) summary
- window: 10m
- p95: 242ms -> 412ms (max)
- p99: 0.91s -> 2.63s (max)
- queue_depth: 0.7 -> 4.8
- config_version header: v58 -> v57 at t+03:12
link: https://perf-canary.redwood/run/rollback-drill-2025-02-12

Sofia Ivanova: okay spike correlates exactly with version flip

Noah Patel: another mitigation: staggered cache invalidation across workers. today rollback controller flips version for the whole scope, and each worker receives new config + flushes immediately. so we get synchronized cold start.

Omar El-Sayed: can we random-jitter the flush (or stage apply across shards) even during rollback?

Sean Gallagher: rollback is expected to be “fast”. but “fast” doesn’t mean “synchronized.” if we can do 30-60s stagger and still mitigate regression, that might be a net win.

Connor O'Brien: Release Eng note: if we add jitter/stagger, make sure rollback API semantics are clear. today people assume “rollback completed” means all workers are on old version. if we do stagger, we need to define “rollback initiated” vs “rollback converged”.

Benji Okafor: control plane can expose status per shard/worker group, but Console currently shows a single state. We can add “converging” state and ETA.

Allison Grant: for alerting: we can gate on “rollback converged” before clearing incident checklists, but also suppress latency gating while state == converging (with limits so we don’t mask real issues).

Noah Patel: I like:
- Phase 1: change config pointer immediately (so new requests start targeting old version)
- Phase 2: worker apply + cache actions are staggered

Omar El-Sayed: careful: if pointer flips, workers that haven’t applied yet are still executing with old/new mismatch? depends on whether config is pulled per request or cached

Noah Patel: right now workers poll + cache config for 30s and also get push notifications. on push they apply.

Noah Patel: we can implement “apply token” (epoch) and have request path include required epoch. if worker is behind, it either self-updates or rejects/redirects.

Sean Gallagher: reject would cause 5xx; redirect would cause retries. both bad in rollback.

Omar El-Sayed: alternative: keep config read-through on request boundaries (cheap) and apply only when safe, but that’s more invasive.

Sofia Ivanova: could we instead keep the immediate apply but make cache flush lazy?

Omar El-Sayed: lazy flush idea:
- keep existing PrefixCache entries but tag with `config_epoch`
- on lookup, only use entries matching current epoch; otherwise treat as miss
- in background, evict old-epoch entries gradually

Noah Patel: that’s good: avoids a “flush storm” and still prevents correctness issues.

Allison Grant: from metrics, the pain is the synchronized cold prefill. lazy eviction would smooth the curve.

Sean Gallagher: would memory blow up (keeping 2 epochs) during rollback?

Omar El-Sayed: worst case yes. but rollback is rare and we can cap: allow at most 2 epochs; when new epoch arrives, start evicting oldest epoch at fixed rate (e.g., X MB/s) until under budget.

Noah Patel: for quant rollbacks, do we need stricter behavior? we can’t reuse KV across weight changes.

Omar El-Sayed: with epoch tagging, KV entries from old weights wouldn’t be used after rollback (epoch mismatch). so correctness ok, but memory overhead may be higher. also performance will still be “cold,” but at least not a synchronized flush event.

Sofia Ivanova: but after rollback, we *want* old epoch entries (the rolled-back version) to be hot again. tagging helps: the entries that match rollback epoch might still exist if we didn’t flush them previously.

Noah Patel: good point. today we flush everything, losing the chance to reuse old cache.

Benji Okafor: can we store both epochs when we canary? so if we roll back, old epoch cache is still there.

Noah Patel: yes. that’s the ideal. rollback should be “go back to known-good” including warmed caches if possible.

Sean Gallagher: operational requirement: rollback should not increase p95 by >25% for more than 2 minutes (rough target). can we hit that with lazy eviction + stagger?

Allison Grant: we can add a panel specifically for “rollback disturbance window” and measure. but please also add a metric: `cache_epoch_active` and `cache_epoch_evictions_total` tagged by worker group.

Noah Patel: action items I’m proposing:
1) Change cache invalidation from “flush now” to epoch-based eligibility (lazy eviction) for PrefixCache and KV reuse pool.
2) Make route hint cache verify compatibility rather than invalidate globally.
3) Add optional stagger/jitter for any remaining “must flush” operations.
4) Update rollback API/Console status wording: initiated vs converged.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. I can take (1) for PrefixCache + KV pool tagging.

Sofia Ivanova: I’ll take (2) for route hints + compat check (scheduler-owned).

Allison Grant: I’ll draft the metrics + dashboard annotation requirements and send to #eng-sre.

Sean Gallagher: pls also update runbook language: “expect small transient latency increase; if sustained > X, pause further changes”

Connor O'Brien: once you have a PR up, tag me. we’ll need to coordinate runtime release train so it lands before limited GA.

Noah Patel: cool. I’ll write up a short design note and link it here. also will sanity-check with Paula/Console team on the “converged” status naming.

Omar El-Sayed: dropping a quick sketch:
```
// on config update
current_epoch = cfg.version_epoch

// cache get
if entry.epoch != current_epoch: miss

// background
evict(epoch != current_epoch) at rate_limit
```

Noah Patel: perfect. also: let’s make sure rollback doesn’t trigger a thundering herd of background eviction threads. one per worker, low-priority.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: 

Allison Grant: :eyes: on metrics tagging: include `config_version` and `rollback_id` if available

Noah Patel: yep. we can propagate rollback_id from control plane event to runtime logs/metrics.

Noah Patel: will follow up with PR links.

Noah Patel: today it’s “trust cache”. we can make it “trust but verify”: if cached hint points to kernel/profile not compatible with current config, recompute.

source.messages:
Noah Patel: heads up for quant profile library runtime wiring — a few concerns I want to sync on:
1) determinism (same request+route should resolve same profile every time)
2) cache interactions (prefix/KV cache + batching) w/ mixed quant modes
3) traces/logs: how do we *prove* profile applied vs fallback

Sofia Ivanova: +1 esp on determinism. if control plane returns `recommended` alias and that changes under us, we’ll have "same config" but different kernels/weights day-to-day.

Jordan Lee: determinism requirement from platform side: resolved settings must be deterministic per route config snapshot. For v1 we should treat `quantization_profile_ref` as pinned (profile_id + version), not a moving alias.

Noah Patel: ok so runtime should only see a fully-resolved `profile_id@version`? (or `disabled`)

Jordan Lee: yep. policy engine resolves route cfg -> concrete profile ref. if alias exists at all it should be resolved at write-time, not request-time.

Mateo Alvarez: strongly prefer that. runtime hot path shouldn’t have to interpret aliases.

Omar El-Sayed: cache stuff: we need to make sure we don’t share KV cache entries across quant mode boundaries. if we have prefix caching keyed just on prompt hash + model_id, enabling INT8 on route would start reusing FP16 KV from earlier traffic :grimacing:

Grace Kim: yup, and also attention kernels behave differently w/ some act quant paths. key must include at least `quant_profile_id` (or "baseline") + maybe some kernel selection hash.

Hiro Tanaka: kernel selection is a function of (gpu sku, seq len, batch shape, quant mode). If we switch quant, the selected kernel path can change. Agree cache key needs `quant_profile_id` and probably `quant_mode` is redundant if profile id is present.

Noah Patel: current cache key (simplified) is:
`cache_key = model_id + tokenizer_rev + prompt_prefix_hash + max_new_tokens_bucket`
We can add `quant_profile_id` and `weights_rev`.

Omar El-Sayed: also watch out for "baseline" not being a real profile id. I suggest we normalize to `quant_profile_id=none` and include that string in the key so we don’t collide.

Sofia Ivanova: batching: if continuous batching mixes requests with different quant profiles on same replica, are we going to be ok? most implementations assume same weights resident.

Hiro Tanaka: mixing profiles in a batch is a no-go if it implies different weights / different quantized weight pack. If profiles are per-model variant and you load one profile per worker, then batch scheduler must bucket by `quant_profile_id`.

Grace Kim: +1. some profiles may change weight packing layout too.

Noah Patel: so options:
A) worker pins to one profile per model variant (baseline or a given profile) and scheduler routes accordingly
B) allow dynamic switching but then you thrash weight loads and blow latency

Mateo Alvarez: A for v1. treat profile selection like model variant selection. runtime: route -> (model_variant, quant_profile_id) -> pool key.

Sofia Ivanova: ok then queue partitioning by pool key. we already do that for model variants.

Omar El-Sayed: memory impact: if we have both baseline + quant workers live, KV cache memory behavior differs (int8 activations can reduce compute but KV often stays fp16/bf16). we should confirm we’re not assuming kv dtype changes.

Hiro Tanaka: current plan: quantization profile affects weights (and maybe activation quant in matmuls), but KV cache dtype remains bf16. so kv memory doesn’t drop materially; decode perf might improve. need to document.

Noah Patel: traces/logs: what’s the best way to surface profile application? We need to answer "did this request run under profile X or fallback?" quickly.

Caleb Johnson: tracing side: we can tag spans with attributes. suggestion:
- `llm.quant.profile_id` (string; "none" for baseline)
- `llm.quant.applied` (true/false)
- `llm.quant.resolution` ("applied" | "fallback" | "disabled")
- `llm.quant.fallback_reason` (enum string; e.g. "incompatible_gpu" "seq_len_exceeded" "kill_switch" "artifact_load_failed")
Also log annotation on request log for sampling.

Logan Wright: metrics too pls. if you emit `fallback_rate{route,model,profile_id,reason}` we can build alerting. but traces are essential for debugging single request IDs.

Grace Kim: for fallback reason, please include kernel guardrails too (e.g. "unsupported_kernel_path") so we can triage quickly.

Noah Patel: where do we decide fallback? policy engine may already do compatibility checks, but runtime can still fail to load artifact.

Jordan Lee: plan is: policy does deterministic compatibility (gpu class, max ctx, model family). runtime still needs runtime-guardrails (artifact fetch failure, checksum mismatch, unexpected precision) -> fallback with reason.

Mateo Alvarez: we should make sure fallback is *single hop* — don’t want loops like: apply profile -> fails -> retry policy -> re-resolves same profile -> fails again.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. runtime should have local "profile load failed" cache with TTL so we don’t hammer registry.

Noah Patel: good call. Implementation thought:
- on request: resolved_profile_ref comes from router
- runtime attempts `ensure_profile_loaded(ref)`
- if error: mark `ref` unhealthy for (say) 60s, emit event, then run baseline weights

Omar El-Sayed: and caches should record which profile ran. otherwise prefix cache might still store entries under the "requested" profile id even if fallback happened.

Noah Patel: yup, cache key should use *effective* profile id (after fallback). So if fallback occurs, set `effective_quant_profile_id=none`.

Caleb Johnson: for trace tagging, use effective too, and add requested separately:
- `llm.quant.requested_profile_id`
- `llm.quant.effective_profile_id`

Grace Kim: +1, otherwise you can’t tell why it didn’t apply.

Hiro Tanaka: kernel selection logs: can we include a compact `kernel_path` tag? even a short enum helps (e.g. `flashattn_v3_int8w` vs `flashattn_v3_fp16`).

Noah Patel: we can put it in debug logs, not sure about always-on trace tags (cardinality). maybe a sampled event.

Logan Wright: agree re cardinality. put kernel path behind debug sampling; but profile_id is ok.

Sofia Ivanova: another determinism angle: if profile artifacts stored in registry change in-place, runtime could load different bits under same id. do we have signing/hash?

Mateo Alvarez: profiles must be immutable by (id, version). registry should serve content-addressed artifacts, or at least enforce immutability w/ checksum validation.

Jordan Lee: registry team is adding signing + versioned artifact storage. runtime validates checksum before using.

Noah Patel: ok. I’ll add to runtime design doc:
- pool key includes profile_id@version
- scheduler buckets by pool key; no mixed-profile batches
- prefix/KV cache key includes effective profile id
- trace tags for requested/effective + resolution + fallback reason
- unhealthy cache to prevent thundering herd on repeated load failures

Omar El-Sayed: please call out interaction w/ prefix cache invalidation: when toggling profile on a route, old prefix cache entries stay but are keyed differently, so safe (but cache hit rate resets). that’s expected.

Sofia Ivanova: and mention latency hit on first request after enable due to profile load. can we prewarm?

Noah Patel: yeah: control plane / console can trigger "preload" by hitting a warmup endpoint or we can lazy-load with a clear warning. v1 probably lazy + doc.

Caleb Johnson: last request: make sure request logs have a single field too. Something like `quant_profile_id=<effective>` and `quant_resolution=<...>` so oncall can grep.

Noah Patel: ack. I’ll coordinate with logging/tracing. thanks all.

Mateo Alvarez: :thumbsup: please drop the draft in #eng-runtime once you’ve updated. want to sanity check before we commit to API shapes.

Noah Patel: will do.

Grace Kim: for fallback reason, please include kernel guardrails too (e.g. "unsupported_kernel_path") so we can triage quickly.

Jordan Lee: plan is: policy does deterministic compatibility (gpu class, max ctx, model family). runtime still needs runtime-guardrails (artifact fetch failure, checksum mismatch, unexpected precision) -> fallback with reason.

Mateo Alvarez: we should make sure fallback is *single hop* — don’t want loops like: apply profile -> fails -> retry policy -> re-resolves same profile -> fails again.

Sofia Ivanova: +1. runtime should have local "profile load failed" cache with TTL so we don’t hammer registry.

Noah Patel: good call. Implementation thought:
- on request: resolved_profile_ref comes from router
- runtime attempts `ensure_profile_loaded(ref)`

- `llm.quant.effective_profile_id`

Grace Kim: +1, otherwise you can’t tell why it didn’t apply.

Noah Patel: we can put it in debug logs, not sure about always-on trace tags (cardinality). maybe a sampled event.

Logan Wright: agree re cardinality. put kernel path behind debug sampling; but profile_id is ok.

Sofia Ivanova: another determinism angle: if profile artifacts stored in registry change in-place, runtime could load different bits under same id. do we have signing/hash?

Mateo Alvarez: profiles must be immutable by (id, version). registry should serve content-addressed artifacts, or at least enforce immutability w/ checksum validation.

Jordan Lee: registry team is adding signing + versioned artifact storage. runtime validates checksum before using.

- pool key includes profile_id@version
- scheduler buckets by pool key; no mixed-profile batches
- prefix/KV cache key includes effective profile id
- trace tags for requested/effective + resolution + fallback reason

Noah Patel: will do.

Noah Patel: heads up for quant profile library runtime wiring — a few concerns I want to sync on:

2) cache interactions (prefix/KV cache + batching) w/ mixed quant modes
3) traces/logs: how do we *prove* profile applied vs fallback

Sofia Ivanova: +1 esp on determinism. if control plane returns `recommended` alias and that changes under us, we’ll have "same config" but different kernels/weights day-to-day.

Jordan Lee: determinism requirement from platform side: resolved settings must be deterministic per route config snapshot. For v1 we should treat `quantization_profile_ref` as pinned (profile_id + version), not a moving alias.

Noah Patel: ok so runtime should only see a fully-resolved `profile_id@version`? (or `disabled`)

Jordan Lee: yep. policy engine resolves route cfg -> concrete profile ref. if alias exists at all it should be resolved at write-time, not request-time.

Mateo Alvarez: strongly prefer that. runtime hot path shouldn’t have to interpret aliases.

Grace Kim: yup, and also attention kernels behave differently w/ some act quant paths. key must include at least `quant_profile_id` (or "baseline") + maybe some kernel selection hash.

Hiro Tanaka: kernel selection is a function of (gpu sku, seq len, batch shape, quant mode). If we switch quant, the selected kernel path can change. Agree cache key needs `quant_profile_id` and probably `quant_mode` is redundant if profile id is present.

`cache_key = model_id + tokenizer_rev + prompt_prefix_hash + max_new_tokens_bucket`
We can add `quant_profile_id` and `weights_rev`.

Sofia Ivanova: batching: if continuous batching mixes requests with different quant profiles on same replica, are we going to be ok? most implementations assume same weights resident.

Hiro Tanaka: mixing profiles in a batch is a no-go if it implies different weights / different quantized weight pack. If profiles are per-model variant and you load one profile per worker, then batch scheduler must bucket by `quant_profile_id`.

Grace Kim: +1. some profiles may change weight packing layout too.

Noah Patel: so options:
A) worker pins to one profile per model variant (baseline or a given profile) and scheduler routes accordingly
B) allow dynamic switching but then you thrash weight loads and blow latency

Mateo Alvarez: A for v1. treat profile selection like model variant selection. runtime: route -> (model_variant, quant_profile_id) -> pool key.

Sofia Ivanova: ok then queue partitioning by pool key. we already do that for model variants.

Omar El-Sayed: memory impact: if we have both baseline + quant workers live, KV cache memory behavior differs (int8 activations can reduce compute but KV often stays fp16/bf16). we should confirm we’re not assuming kv dtype changes.

- `llm.quant.resolution` ("applied" | "fallback" | "disabled")
- `llm.quant.fallback_reason` (enum string; e.g. "incompatible_gpu" "seq_len_exceeded" "kill_switch" "artifact_load_failed")

source.text:
Dev Patel: anyone remember the gotchas when adding a new upstream tokenizer to hosted? qwen2.5 looks fine in eval harness but I’m worried about stop sequences + special tokens in streaming

Ethan Park: yeah, two things we’ve hit: (1) `eos_token_id` mismatch when upstream publishes multiple eos candidates, and (2) our OpenAI-compat path strips leading spaces differently. check the tokenizer config in registry and make sure we set `strip_leading_space=false` if needed

Aisha Rahman: also for evals, run the internal prompt set with the *exact* stop sequence config we ship. we had a false pass last time because harness used default stops but prod route used `

` + `</s>`

Connor OBrien: rollout side: please ensure router policy has fallback #2 to a cross-family safe default (we’ve been using `rw/llama-3.1-70b-instruct`), otherwise incident response is painful if the whole family hits OOM

Maya Srinivasan: +1. and don’t forget to set max_context error message. we had complaints when it returned generic 500 instead of a clean 400 w/ actionable text

Dev Patel: perfect. I’ll update registry PR description w/ shipped stop sequences + set the fallback chain. thanks
