source.messages:
Jada Williams: Been digging into the latest blended $/1M output token spikes on a couple long-context routes. We’re seeing the usual pattern: output tokens flat-ish, but per-request GPU time up because prefill is huge. We need a *runtime* signal that explains this better than “avg input tokens”.

Jada Williams: Proposal: add a “prefill/decode ratio” metric (or prefill share) emitted per request and aggregated hourly. It would help explain unit cost shifts when prompts get longer or cache hit rate changes.

Allison Grant: +1. For the dashboard folks keep asking “why did $/output token jump when output length didn’t change” and the answer is almost always prefill dominated.

Logan Wright: Agree. But we should be super explicit on definition so we don’t end up with 3 different ratios.

Nadia Rahman: What exactly do you mean by prefill/decode ratio here? Tokens? time? FLOPs proxy?

Jada Williams: Two options:
1) Token-based proxy: `prefill_tokens / max(output_tokens, 1)` (where prefill_tokens = input tokens actually processed in prefill)
2) Time-based: `prefill_ms / max(decode_ms, 1)`

Jada Williams: I lean time-based for cost correlation, but token-based is easier to make consistent across models/hw. Maybe we emit both (but only chart one).

Felix Schneider: If we do token-based, we need to be careful: prefill tokens != “input tokens” when prefix/KV cache hits. Ideally it’s “uncached prefill tokens”.

Nadia Rahman: Yep. If we already have prefix cache instrumentation we can compute effective prefill tokens as `input_tokens - cached_prefix_tokens` (or whatever the runtime calls it). Otherwise token-based will look bad for cached traffic.

Quentin Harris: From model metadata side: different models have different prefill/decode perf characteristics. A time-based ratio is comparable within a model/version, less so across models. Token ratio is more apples-to-apples but less tied to $$.

Allison Grant: What does the unit economics dashboard actually need?

Logan Wright: For v1 dashboard we just need an explanatory signal that moves with $/output token. Finance will keep living on blended $/1M output tokens, but we want a “drivers” panel. If prefill dominates, we can say “long context / low output length / cache regression.”

Caleb Johnson: Also for data quality, time-based metrics might be sampled/dropped. Token counts are already part of metering so they’re more reliable.

Sean Gallagher: +1 from SRE: ms-level per-request metrics can get noisy depending on sampling, p99 spikes, etc. If we add a new thing, please make sure we can SLO it or at least detect if it goes missing.

Jada Williams: Makes sense. How about:
- Primary: `effective_prefill_tokens_per_output_token = effective_prefill_tokens / max(output_tokens, 1)`
- Secondary (optional): `prefill_ms_per_output_token = prefill_ms / max(output_tokens, 1)`

Jada Williams: That maps directly to “cost per output token goes up when you spend more prefill per output token.”

Nadia Rahman: Naming matters. “prefill/decode ratio” is ambiguous because decode tokens == output tokens but decode time != output tokens. Suggest we call it what it is:
- `prefill_tokens_per_output_token`
- `prefill_ms_per_output_token`

Nadia Rahman: Then the dashboard can label it “Prefill work per output token” or similar.

Felix Schneider: Implementation detail: where do we get effective_prefill_tokens? Runtime currently emits:
- `input_tokens`
- `output_tokens`
- `prefix_cache_hit` (bool)
- `prefix_cache_hit_tokens` (I think we have this only on the new path)

Felix Schneider: We should standardize the field names. Otherwise aggregation will be a mess.

Logan Wright: If runtime can emit `effective_input_tokens` (aka uncached tokens processed in prefill), that’s clean for the pipeline. We can still keep raw input tokens for other analyses.

Allison Grant: And for the caching story, we can do a decomposition:
- input_tokens
- effective_prefill_tokens
- output_tokens
- cache hit rate
Then we can see if the ratio went up because prompts got longer vs cache got worse.

Quentin Harris: One more edge case: tool calling / structured output. Sometimes “output tokens” can be low but decode is still heavy because of stop/start? Not sure. Time-based metric would catch it.

Nadia Rahman: Also streaming vs non-streaming shouldn’t change the ratio itself, but streaming paths have had output token parity issues historically. If we add anything that divides by output_tokens, missing output_tokens will explode the ratio.

Caleb Johnson: +1000. If output_tokens is undercounted, prefill_tokens_per_output_token will look like a regression when it’s actually metering. We need a guardrail: if output_tokens < X or null -> emit null ratio (or clamp) and flag coverage.

Sean Gallagher: Prefer emitting numerator/denominator separately and let the warehouse compute ratio with safe divide. That also makes it easier to alert on missing denominators.

Jada Williams: Works for me. Runtime emits:
- `runtime_prefill_tokens_effective` (int)
- `runtime_output_tokens` (int) (already)
Optionally later:
- `runtime_prefill_ms` and `runtime_decode_ms`

Jada Williams: Then aggregation computes `safe_divide(sum(prefill_tokens_effective), sum(output_tokens))` per {model, model_version, region, tier, quant_profile, streaming_flag?}.

Logan Wright: +1. Keep dimensions minimal. If we add this to the economics rollups, we’ll chart by model/region/tier and show it as a driver.

Allison Grant: For UI: I’ll talk to Alicia, but I think we can put it under “Workload composition” next to avg output length + cache hit rate.

Felix Schneider: What about long-context specifically: should we bucket by context length? That might blow up cardinality.

Nadia Rahman: Please no buckets in telemetry tags. But in the warehouse we can compute percentiles of input_tokens and join. For now just ratio + maybe a simple “avg input tokens” is enough.

Jada Williams: Action items:
- I’ll write up the metric definition + runtime field names and post in the unit economics signal doc.
- @Nadia Rahman can you sanity check what we can emit across both serving paths (classic + new streaming)?
- @Logan Wright can you confirm where you want the fields in the metering schema (so Caleb can add DQ checks)?

Nadia Rahman: Yep. I’ll audit current runtime event payloads. If prefix_cache_hit_tokens is missing on some paths, we can still emit effective_prefill_tokens = input_tokens when unknown (but then it won’t attribute cache savings). Better than nothing, but we should add a “effective_prefill_tokens_source=estimated|measured” flag? (maybe too much).

Caleb Johnson: If you do that, make sure dashboard doesn’t mix estimated/measured silently. Even a single boolean “effective_prefill_tokens_measured” could let us track rollout coverage.

Sean Gallagher: From ops side, measured-vs-estimated is fine if we can alert when measured coverage drops. Otherwise we’ll be debugging phantom regressions again.

Logan Wright: Let’s keep it simple: emit `effective_prefill_tokens` and `effective_prefill_tokens_is_measured` (bool). Aggregation will compute ratio only for measured by default and separately track coverage.

Allison Grant: That plays nicely with the “confidence indicator” idea we’ve been discussing for dashboard panels.

Jada Williams: Cool. I’ll incorporate. Thanks all.

Nadia Rahman: Please also include a note in the definition: why we normalize by *output tokens* (because unit metric is $/1M output tokens), and common interpretations:
- ratio up + cache hit flat => longer prompts / context growth
- ratio up + cache hit down => cache regression
- ratio down => more output length or better caching

Jada Williams: Will do.

Quentin Harris: If you want a quick validation set: we have a couple routes that are known long-context (summarization + retrieval). I can send example model IDs + routes for testing.

Felix Schneider: dropping here: for runtime naming I’d prefer `prefill_tokens_uncached` over “effective”, but I’m fine either way as long as we document.

Jada Williams: Ack. I’ll pick one and be consistent in the doc + PR.

Sean Gallagher: Add a runbook snippet too: “if prefill_tokens_per_output_token spikes, check output token metering completeness first.”

Caleb Johnson: :thumbsup: I’ll add a DQ rule: if output_tokens drops >30% for a model/region while request_count stable, flag as metering issue, and suppress ratio alerts.

Allison Grant: Great. Keep me posted when we have a first sample chart; would love to include in next dogfood update.

text:
Jada Williams: Been digging into the latest blended $/1M output token spikes on a couple long-context routes. We’re seeing the usual pattern: output tokens flat-ish, but per-request GPU time up because prefill is huge. We need a *runtime* signal that explains this better than “avg input tokens”.

Jada Williams: Proposal: add a “prefill/decode ratio” metric (or prefill share) emitted per request and aggregated hourly. It would help explain unit cost shifts when prompts get longer or cache hit rate changes.

Allison Grant: +1. For the dashboard folks keep asking “why did $/output token jump when output length didn’t change” and the answer is almost always prefill dominated.

Logan Wright: Agree. But we should be super explicit on definition so we don’t end up with 3 different ratios.

Nadia Rahman: What exactly do you mean by prefill/decode ratio here? Tokens? time? FLOPs proxy?

Jada Williams: Two options:
1) Token-based proxy: `prefill_tokens / max(output_tokens, 1)` (where prefill_tokens = input tokens actually processed in prefill)
2) Time-based: `prefill_ms / max(decode_ms, 1)`

Jada Williams: I lean time-based for cost correlation, but token-based is easier to make consistent across models/hw. Maybe we emit both (but only chart one).

Felix Schneider: If we do token-based, we need to be careful: prefill tokens != “input tokens” when prefix/KV cache hits. Ideally it’s “uncached prefill tokens”.

Nadia Rahman: Yep. If we already have prefix cache instrumentation we can compute effective prefill tokens as `input_tokens - cached_prefix_tokens` (or whatever the runtime calls it). Otherwise token-based will look bad for cached traffic.

Quentin Harris: From model metadata side: different models have different prefill/decode perf characteristics. A time-based ratio is comparable within a model/version, less so across models. Token ratio is more apples-to-apples but less tied to $$.

Allison Grant: What does the unit economics dashboard actually need?

Logan Wright: For v1 dashboard we just need an explanatory signal that moves with $/output token. Finance will keep living on blended $/1M output tokens, but we want a “drivers” panel. If prefill dominates, we can say “long context / low output length / cache regression.”

Caleb Johnson: Also for data quality, time-based metrics might be sampled/dropped. Token counts are already part of metering so they’re more reliable.

Sean Gallagher: +1 from SRE: ms-level per-request metrics can get noisy depending on sampling, p99 spikes, etc. If we add a new thing, please make sure we can SLO it or at least detect if it goes missing.

Jada Williams: Makes sense. How about:
- Primary: `effective_prefill_tokens_per_output_token = effective_prefill_tokens / max(output_tokens, 1)`
- Secondary (optional): `prefill_ms_per_output_token = prefill_ms / max(output_tokens, 1)`

Jada Williams: That maps directly to “cost per output token goes up when you spend more prefill per output token.”

Nadia Rahman: Naming matters. “prefill/decode ratio” is ambiguous because decode tokens == output tokens but decode time != output tokens. Suggest we call it what it is:
- `prefill_tokens_per_output_token`
- `prefill_ms_per_output_token`

Nadia Rahman: Then the dashboard can label it “Prefill work per output token” or similar.

Felix Schneider: Implementation detail: where do we get effective_prefill_tokens? Runtime currently emits:
- `input_tokens`
- `output_tokens`
- `prefix_cache_hit` (bool)
- `prefix_cache_hit_tokens` (I think we have this only on the new path)

Felix Schneider: We should standardize the field names. Otherwise aggregation will be a mess.

Logan Wright: If runtime can emit `effective_input_tokens` (aka uncached tokens processed in prefill), that’s clean for the pipeline. We can still keep raw input tokens for other analyses.

Allison Grant: And for the caching story, we can do a decomposition:
- input_tokens
- effective_prefill_tokens
- output_tokens
- cache hit rate
Then we can see if the ratio went up because prompts got longer vs cache got worse.

Quentin Harris: One more edge case: tool calling / structured output. Sometimes “output tokens” can be low but decode is still heavy because of stop/start? Not sure. Time-based metric would catch it.

Nadia Rahman: Also streaming vs non-streaming shouldn’t change the ratio itself, but streaming paths have had output token parity issues historically. If we add anything that divides by output_tokens, missing output_tokens will explode the ratio.

Caleb Johnson: +1000. If output_tokens is undercounted, prefill_tokens_per_output_token will look like a regression when it’s actually metering. We need a guardrail: if output_tokens < X or null -> emit null ratio (or clamp) and flag coverage.

Sean Gallagher: Prefer emitting numerator/denominator separately and let the warehouse compute ratio with safe divide. That also makes it easier to alert on missing denominators.

Jada Williams: Works for me. Runtime emits:
- `runtime_prefill_tokens_effective` (int)
- `runtime_output_tokens` (int) (already)
Optionally later:
- `runtime_prefill_ms` and `runtime_decode_ms`

Jada Williams: Then aggregation computes `safe_divide(sum(prefill_tokens_effective), sum(output_tokens))` per {model, model_version, region, tier, quant_profile, streaming_flag?}.

Logan Wright: +1. Keep dimensions minimal. If we add this to the economics rollups, we’ll chart by model/region/tier and show it as a driver.

Allison Grant: For UI: I’ll talk to Alicia, but I think we can put it under “Workload composition” next to avg output length + cache hit rate.

Felix Schneider: What about long-context specifically: should we bucket by context length? That might blow up cardinality.

Nadia Rahman: Please no buckets in telemetry tags. But in the warehouse we can compute percentiles of input_tokens and join. For now just ratio + maybe a simple “avg input tokens” is enough.

- ratio up + cache hit down => cache regression
- ratio down => more output length or better caching

Jada Williams: Will do.

Jada Williams: Ack. I’ll pick one and be consistent in the doc + PR.

Mateo Rivera: quick heads-up — after bumping checkpoint -> model@v3.4.2 on staging we're seeing a sustained p99 latency jump (~+22%). Started at 08:12 UTC when the canary traffic hit the new image.
Alex Chen: saw that too, logs show a lot of kernel re-selection churn and kv cache evictions. attaching a quick tail from the runtime log: 
```
kernel_selector: prefer=optimized_attention_v2 (was optimized_attention_v1)
kv_cache: size=1.2G evictions=4237 hits=0.04 misses=0.96
prefetch.policy: stride=4 -> stride=1 (policy flip)
```
Priya Singh: interesting — the prefetch stride flip looks like the new kernel heuristic landed in 7a3f2d0 (merge). That commit changed the prefetch policy thresholds during model onboards.
Li Zhang: profiling snapshot (staging, sample window 08:20–08:28): continuous batching decreased, more frequent small batches -> worse kernel throughput. might be a side-effect of stride=1 causing more KV cache thrash.
Jordan Blake: ci-bot just posted a perf-check failure for the PR that triggered the bump: 
ci-bot: perf-check: p99_latency delta +18.7% (threshold 10%) — FAIL. See /ci/perf/1738443.
deploy-bot: rollout paused for canary group-2 by automations (policy: perf-regression-detected).
Alex Chen: I started a local bisect against the onboard flow. early pointer: the kernel-selector refactor in 7a3f2d0 toggles prefetch when tokenizer.length-dist > 1024. For short inputs that now forces a different kernel and stride.
Mateo Rivera: can we reproduce with a synthetic workload? I can spin up a perf harness that mimics our chat traffic 1–16 tokens.
Priya Singh: please do. I'll also try reverting just the prefetch change in a staging image and run the canary. If latency drops we can either revert or pin kernel selection for v3.4.2.
Li Zhang: quick debug note: KV cache metrics show cold misses on repeated prefixes — suggests our prefix/KV key normalization changed during the tokenizer bump in the checkpoint. The tokenizer mapping delta could cause higher effective sequence variety, hurting cache reuse.
Alex Chen: command I ran locally: `./scripts/run_harness --model model@v3.4.2 --staging --qps 120 --seq-dist small` produced p99 ~ 480ms vs baseline 390ms. Adding `--force-prefetch-stride=4` drops p99 back to ~395ms.
Priya Singh: good signal. I'll open an emergency PR to revert the prefetch threshold change and add a guard: only flip stride when median_seq_len >= 512 AND kv_hit_rate >= 0.6.
Jordan Blake: filing rollback ticket now and flagging dedicated owners: runtime/kernel-team, onboards. Also posting an incident-lite note.
Mateo Rivera: action items: 1) Priya revert PR and canary test (Priya). 2) Alex finish bisect to isolate exact sub-change in 7a3f2d0 affecting tokenizer->prefetch interaction (Alex). 3) Li collect KV hit/miss histograms for last 24h and attach to ticket (Li). 4) Jordan coordinate rollout pause + stakeholder comms (Jordan).
ci-bot: note: any new image with the revert will re-run perf-check automatically once pushed.
deploy-bot: canary remains paused. manual override requires triage call.
Mateo Rivera: thanks all — let's keep updates in this thread. If we confirm revert fixes perf, we can do a quick patch release and unblock the canary.
Alex Chen: update in ~30m with bisect result.
Priya Singh: I'll draft the revert PR now.
Li Zhang: uploading KV histograms to the ticket (link: https://redwood.internal/ops/tickets/6123).
Mateo Rivera: perfect — moving to async. :thumbsup:

source.messages:
Nadia Rahman: we need a batching utilization metric out of runtime asap. looking at the early unit econ views, we’re seeing $/1M output tokens drift but we can’t tell if it’s “more expensive GPUs” vs “worse batching”. cache hit rate helps a bit but doesn’t explain the spikes.

Nadia Rahman: asking eng-runtime: can we add a stable metric for “how full were we” during decode/prefill? something finance/product can interpret w/o reading kernel traces.

Logan Wright: +1. in pipeline we can join whatever you emit, but right now we only have request-level batch_size-ish from gateway logs, and that doesn’t reflect continuous batching behavior.

Allison Grant: what exactly do you mean by utilization? avg batch size? gpu occupancy? those are v diff and can be misleading.

Nadia Rahman: fair. not asking for GPU occupancy (too noisy + hardware-specific). i want something like “tokens processed per step / max tokens possible per step given configured limits”.

Sean Gallagher: SRE angle: please keep cardinality sane. if we tag this with model_version + route + customer we’ll melt prometheus. need a constrained label set.

Jada Williams: we already have internal counters in the scheduler for:
- num_active_seqs per engine tick
- max_seqs configured
- decode_tokens_this_tick
- prefill_tokens_this_tick
- max_tokens_per_tick (depends on block size + max_num_batched_tokens)

Jada Williams: we could export a gauge (or sampled histogram) for “batch_tokens_utilization = (prefill_tokens + decode_tokens) / max_tokens_per_tick”. and also export seq_utilization = num_active_seqs / max_seqs.

Felix Schneider: if you do both, dashboards can separate “we had lots of sequences but low tokens” vs “we had long sequences but seq slots empty”.

Allison Grant: for continuous batching, max_tokens_per_tick is not a constant unless you’re explicitly enforcing a per-step token budget. do we have that?

Jada Williams: yes in runtime config: `scheduler.max_batched_tokens` (hard cap per tick). today it’s per engine instance, same value across a pool.

Jada Williams: caveat: during decode we often hit `max_seqs` before `max_batched_tokens` depending on avg output length. so the two utilization metrics help.

Caleb Johnson: from economics side, we also need to know how to interpret “utilization < 1”. e.g. if utilization low because traffic low vs because scheduler leaving gaps. can we add a “queue_depth” or “pending_requests” metric too?

Sean Gallagher: +1 queue depth helps when correlating with throttling/autoscale events.

Nadia Rahman: minimum set i’d like:
1) batch_tokens_utilization (0..1)
2) batch_seq_utilization (0..1)
3) queue_depth (int)
optionally split prefill vs decode.

Mei Lin: if you split prefill/decode, please name them deterministically so we don’t get 8 variants. also the warehouse job can store them as columns if stable.

Felix Schneider: proposal for metric names (prom-style):
- `rw_runtime_batch_tokens_utilization` (gauge)
- `rw_runtime_batch_seqs_utilization` (gauge)
- `rw_runtime_scheduler_queue_depth` (gauge)

Felix Schneider: and optionally:
- `rw_runtime_batch_prefill_tokens_utilization`
- `rw_runtime_batch_decode_tokens_utilization`

Allison Grant: gauges sampled every tick could be a lot. what’s tick rate? 10ms-ish? don’t ship that raw.

Jada Williams: tick rate is ~20-50ms depending on load. but we can aggregate inside runtime and flush 10s intervals:
- keep running sum(util)
- count(samples)
- export avg over interval
also export p50/p90? (histogram) might be heavy.

Sean Gallagher: avg over 10s is fine. histograms are expensive but could be okay if label set is tiny.

Logan Wright: for unit economics, avg is actually good. we’ll roll up hourly anyway. a “utilization_avg” and “utilization_p10” would be nice but not required v1.

Nadia Rahman: label set suggestion (please push back):
- `region`
- `cluster` (or capacity_pool)
- `model_id` (normalized, not marketing alias)
- `quantization_profile` (from allowed set)
NO customer_id, no request_route, no model_version (unless we can bucket).

Sean Gallagher: region + cluster + model_id + quant_profile already gets large, but manageable if model_id is normalized and quant_profile is constrained. please do not include raw model_version; if needed, we can map versions in warehouse from model metadata tables.

Allison Grant: does runtime even know region? it knows “pool name” / “colo”. we can add a “deployment_region” constant at startup.

Jada Williams: runtime can emit `pool` and `engine_type` (e.g., vllm-ish vs our custom engine). engine_type may matter.

Caleb Johnson: engine_type could be valuable for debugging regressions when we change scheduler behavior.

Nadia Rahman: ok include `engine` as label (small set).

Felix Schneider: schema detail: utilization definition should be precise or people will argue about it in exec readouts.

Felix Schneider: draft definitions:
- `batch_tokens_utilization = (prefill_tokens_processed + decode_tokens_processed) / max_batched_tokens_configured` averaged over 10s window
- `batch_seqs_utilization = active_sequences / max_num_seqs_configured` averaged over 10s window
- `queue_depth = num_waiting_requests` sampled (avg) over 10s

Allison Grant: one more nuance: `active_sequences` includes sequences that are “paused” due to kv cache eviction/backpressure? depends on scheduler state.

Jada Williams: in our scheduler, active means “in-flight and occupying a slot”. paused still occupies. so seq_utilization is about slot pressure, not GPU work.

Nadia Rahman: that’s fine; in fact it helps explain cases where seq slots are full but tokens_util is low (lots of tiny decodes).

Sean Gallagher: please add a short comment in code / docs: “seq utilization is scheduler slot utilization, not GPU utilization”. otherwise this gets misused.

Logan Wright: timeline? we need this before we freeze the unit econ dashboard v1 definitions.

Jada Williams: i can take this. implementation plan:
- add in scheduler stats collector (10s window)
- export via existing metrics sink
- add unit tests for aggregation window
- verify label cardinality guardrails (allowed model_id/quant_profile)

Jada Williams: can land behind a feature flag in runtime config and enable on 1-2 pools first.

Allison Grant: please also expose max configs as info metrics (e.g. `rw_runtime_scheduler_max_batched_tokens`) so dashboards can sanity check when configs change.

Felix Schneider: +1. changes in `max_batched_tokens` will look like utilization changes if not visible.

Nadia Rahman: awesome. can you also post a tiny example payload so telemetry can map it cleanly?

Jada Williams: sure, sketch:
```
# labels: region, pool, model_id, quantization_profile, engine
rw_runtime_batch_tokens_utilization{region="us-east",pool="hosted-a10",model_id="llama3_70b",quantization_profile="fp16",engine="rw-engine"} 0.72
rw_runtime_batch_prefill_tokens_utilization{...} 0.61
rw_runtime_batch_decode_tokens_utilization{...} 0.79
rw_runtime_batch_seqs_utilization{...} 0.88
rw_runtime_scheduler_queue_depth{...} 14
rw_runtime_scheduler_max_batched_tokens{...} 4096
rw_runtime_scheduler_max_seqs{...} 256
```

Sean Gallagher: looks good. just confirm we’re not adding pool_id that includes shard numbers (those explode cardinality).

Jada Williams: we’ll use pool name without shard. (cluster-wide constant)

Mei Lin: once this lands, please ping me/logan. we’ll add to the economics model as “batching efficiency” signals and wire to drilldowns.

Caleb Johnson: also note for the dashboard: we’ll likely show utilization as % with a tooltip explaining definition + window.

Nadia Rahman: thanks all. action items:
- @Jada Williams implement + enable on 2 pools
- @Sean Gallagher review label set/cardinality
- @Logan Wright/@Mei Lin plan ingestion + join to unit econ aggregates

Nadia Rahman: will follow up with a short confluence note so we have a canonical definition.

Sean Gallagher: :thumbsup: pls include in the note the “not GPU utilization” disclaimer and the 10s aggregation window.

Jada Williams: ack. i’ll open a runtime ticket and link here once up.

Allison Grant: one last ask: can we include a “dropped_due_to_budget” counter? i.e., how often requests wait because max_batched_tokens is hit.

Jada Williams: good idea but might be v1.1. for now queue_depth + utilization should capture it. will add a TODO.

Logan Wright: works. we can correlate queue_depth spikes with utilization pegged at ~1 to infer budget saturation.

Nadia Rahman: closing the loop later this week after first rollout.

text:
Nadia Rahman: we need a batching utilization metric out of runtime asap. looking at the early unit econ views, we’re seeing $/1M output tokens drift but we can’t tell if it’s “more expensive GPUs” vs “worse batching”. cache hit rate helps a bit but doesn’t explain the spikes.

Nadia Rahman: asking eng-runtime: can we add a stable metric for “how full were we” during decode/prefill? something finance/product can interpret w/o reading kernel traces.

Logan Wright: +1. in pipeline we can join whatever you emit, but right now we only have request-level batch_size-ish from gateway logs, and that doesn’t reflect continuous batching behavior.

Allison Grant: what exactly do you mean by utilization? avg batch size? gpu occupancy? those are v diff and can be misleading.

Nadia Rahman: fair. not asking for GPU occupancy (too noisy + hardware-specific). i want something like “tokens processed per step / max tokens possible per step given configured limits”.

Sean Gallagher: SRE angle: please keep cardinality sane. if we tag this with model_version + route + customer we’ll melt prometheus. need a constrained label set.

Jada Williams: we already have internal counters in the scheduler for:
- num_active_seqs per engine tick
- max_seqs configured
- decode_tokens_this_tick
- prefill_tokens_this_tick
- max_tokens_per_tick (depends on block size + max_num_batched_tokens)

Jada Williams: we could export a gauge (or sampled histogram) for “batch_tokens_utilization = (prefill_tokens + decode_tokens) / max_tokens_per_tick”. and also export seq_utilization = num_active_seqs / max_seqs.

Felix Schneider: if you do both, dashboards can separate “we had lots of sequences but low tokens” vs “we had long sequences but seq slots empty”.

Allison Grant: for continuous batching, max_tokens_per_tick is not a constant unless you’re explicitly enforcing a per-step token budget. do we have that?

Jada Williams: yes in runtime config: `scheduler.max_batched_tokens` (hard cap per tick). today it’s per engine instance, same value across a pool.

Jada Williams: caveat: during decode we often hit `max_seqs` before `max_batched_tokens` depending on avg output length. so the two utilization metrics help.

Caleb Johnson: from economics side, we also need to know how to interpret “utilization < 1”. e.g. if utilization low because traffic low vs because scheduler leaving gaps. can we add a “queue_depth” or “pending_requests” metric too?

Sean Gallagher: +1 queue depth helps when correlating with throttling/autoscale events.

Nadia Rahman: minimum set i’d like:
1) batch_tokens_utilization (0..1)
2) batch_seq_utilization (0..1)
3) queue_depth (int)
optionally split prefill vs decode.

Mei Lin: if you split prefill/decode, please name them deterministically so we don’t get 8 variants. also the warehouse job can store them as columns if stable.

Felix Schneider: proposal for metric names (prom-style):
- `rw_runtime_batch_tokens_utilization` (gauge)
- `rw_runtime_batch_seqs_utilization` (gauge)
- `rw_runtime_scheduler_queue_depth` (gauge)

Felix Schneider: and optionally:
- `rw_runtime_batch_prefill_tokens_utilization`
- `rw_runtime_batch_decode_tokens_utilization`

Allison Grant: gauges sampled every tick could be a lot. what’s tick rate? 10ms-ish? don’t ship that raw.

Jada Williams: tick rate is ~20-50ms depending on load. but we can aggregate inside runtime and flush 10s intervals:
- keep running sum(util)
- count(samples)
- export avg over interval
also export p50/p90? (histogram) might be heavy.

Sean Gallagher: avg over 10s is fine. histograms are expensive but could be okay if label set is tiny.

Logan Wright: for unit economics, avg is actually good. we’ll roll up hourly anyway. a “utilization_avg” and “utilization_p10” would be nice but not required v1.

Nadia Rahman: label set suggestion (please push back):
- `region`
- `cluster` (or capacity_pool)
- `model_id` (normalized, not marketing alias)
- `quantization_profile` (from allowed set)
NO customer_id, no request_route, no model_version (unless we can bucket).

Sean Gallagher: region + cluster + model_id + quant_profile already gets large, but manageable if model_id is normalized and quant_profile is constrained. please do not include raw model_version; if needed, we can map versions in warehouse from model metadata tables.

Allison Grant: does runtime even know region? it knows “pool name” / “colo”. we can add a “deployment_region” constant at startup.

Jada Williams: runtime can emit `pool` and `engine_type` (e.g., vllm-ish vs our custom engine). engine_type may matter.

Caleb Johnson: engine_type could be valuable for debugging regressions when we change scheduler behavior.

Nadia Rahman: ok include `engine` as label (small set).

Felix Schneider: schema detail: utilization definition should be precise or people will argue about it in exec readouts.

Felix Schneider: draft definitions:
- `batch_tokens_utilization = (prefill_tokens_processed + decode_tokens_processed) / max_batched_tokens_configured` averaged over 10s window
- `batch_seqs_utilization = active_sequences / max_num_seqs_configured` averaged over 10s window
- `queue_depth = num_waiting_requests` sampled (avg) over 10s

Allison Grant: one more nuance: `active_sequences` includes sequences that are “paused” due to kv cache eviction/backpressure? depends on scheduler state.

Jada Williams: in our scheduler, active means “in-flight and occupying a slot”. paused still occupies. so seq_utilization is about slot pressure, not GPU work.

Nadia Rahman: that’s fine; in fact it helps explain cases where seq slots are full but tokens_util is low (lots of tiny decodes).

Sean Gallagher: please add a short comment in code / docs: “seq utilization is scheduler slot utilization, not GPU utilization”. otherwise this gets misused.

Logan Wright: timeline? we need this before we freeze the unit econ dashboard v1 definitions.

Jada Williams: i can take this. implementation plan:
- add in scheduler stats collector (10s window)
- export via existing metrics sink
- add unit tests for aggregation window
- verify label cardinality guardrails (allowed model_id/quant_profile)

Jada Williams: can land behind a feature flag in runtime config and enable on 1-2 pools first.

Allison Grant: please also expose max configs as info metrics (e.g. `rw_runtime_scheduler_max_batched_tokens`) so dashboards can sanity check when configs change.

Felix Schneider: +1. changes in `max_batched_tokens` will look like utilization changes if not visible.

source.body:
Diego Santos: quick q for audit logging coverage — what runtime-level actions are we calling “sensitive”? thinking model enable/disable, fleet pool bindings, etc. do we need audit events for these or do we treat as internal ops?

Hanae Suzuki: +1, security side assumption has been: any action that changes (a) what model can serve traffic, (b) where traffic can go, or (c) who can access capacity => sensitive.

Marcus Lin: yep. also anything that changes policy enforcement (eg bypassing allow-lists), even if done via runtime config.

Ethan Park: to clarify, “runtime-level” here = changes in serving layer config (model registry flags, pool allocations) vs control plane policy? where are these changes initiated today?

Diego Santos: mix. some via control plane (rollouts/policy), but some are “runtime admin” endpoints (mostly used by oncall) for: 
- enable/disable model version in a region
- bind/unbind model to a GPU pool (fleet pool)
- adjust max_batch_tokens / concurrency caps
- toggle a “degraded mode” flag when a model is flaky

Allison Grant: from observability standards POV, anything done through an “admin endpoint” should emit an audit event unless we explicitly waive it. otherwise we’ll have blind spots during incidents.

Jordan Lee: + for routing-related: if runtime config can change routing weights / fallback order, that’s definitely sensitive. even if it’s “just” to fix latency.

Diego Santos: what about pure performance knobs like continuous batching parameters? those can impact cost/latency but not access control.

Hanae Suzuki: still can be sensitive if it materially changes customer experience or could be abused to DoS. but we can scope it as “sensitive: availability/controls” vs “non-sensitive: tuning” if we have a clear line.

Marcus Lin: line I’d propose: audit any “state change” that affects traffic eligibility or capacity allocation at tenant/project/model level. not every per-host tunable.

Logan Wright: for coverage dashboards, we need a crisp list of expected event_types. otherwise runtime will be a constant “unknown unknowns” bucket.

Nadia Rahman: also cardinality note: don’t dump giant config blobs. if we log diffs, keep it bounded (eg allow-listed keys + before/after strings truncated).

Ethan Park: can we enumerate the runtime actions that are user-initiated vs automatic? eg autoscaler increasing replicas shouldn’t spam audit logs.

Diego Santos: good call. categories:
1) operator-initiated (oncall / console admin)
2) system-initiated (autoscaler, health manager)

Diego Santos: system-initiated shouldn’t be audit, but should be normal telemetry/events.

Hanae Suzuki: agree. audit logs are about accountability. still, system-initiated changes that affect customer traffic might be useful for investigations, but that’s more “operational event stream” than “audit”.

Allison Grant: +1. audit events require actor semantics; “actor.type=service” is ok, but we should avoid turning every reconciliation loop into audit noise.

Marcus Lin: but there are a couple “system actions” we might want to treat as audit-adjacent: if the system disables a model due to safety/abuse detection or flips a policy gate.

Jordan Lee: that’s more policy engine / trust & safety domain, but yes: if runtime auto-disables a model version due to repeated bad outputs, that’s a big deal.

Diego Santos: ok, propose “sensitive runtime actions” list:
- model version enable
- model version disable
- model version pin/unpin to traffic (ie “eligible_for_routing”)
- fleet pool binding create/update/delete (model <-> pool)
- manual capacity override on pool (eg reserved slots, min replicas) when it changes tenant-visible throughput
- enabling/disabling “degraded mode” / “circuit breaker” for a model if it changes routing eligibility

Diego Santos: NOT sensitive (telemetry only):
- per-host kernel selection, compilation cache, warmup
- autoscaler scale up/down
- dynamic batcher tuning unless it changes a hard cap / quota

Hanae Suzuki: looks right. re: batching: if you change max_concurrency / max_inflight for a tenant, that can be used to throttle. I’d call those sensitive when scoped to tenant/project.

Diego Santos: fair. we do have an endpoint to set “tenant concurrency cap override” during incidents.

Marcus Lin: that’s absolutely sensitive. it’s basically a quota override.

Ethan Park: that might be owned by gateway/control plane though? but if runtime applies it directly, audit should still fire at the point of change.

Logan Wright: for coverage: we’ll want event_type names that are stable and map to taxonomy. can runtime align with the registry PR (ENG-4822) rather than inventing new strings?

Allison Grant: please. also include correlation fields: request_id/trace_id + admin_action_id so we can connect a console click -> control plane -> runtime.

Nadia Rahman: and include “deployment_mode” (hosted/dedicated/private) consistently. we’ve seen private missing these fields.

Diego Santos: ok. I’ll sync with eng-security on naming. tentative event_types:
- runtime.model_version.enabled
- runtime.model_version.disabled
- runtime.model_version.routing_eligibility.updated
- runtime.fleet_pool_binding.created
- runtime.fleet_pool_binding.deleted
- runtime.tenant_concurrency_override.updated

Hanae Suzuki: + include target fields (model_id, model_version, pool_id, tenant_id/project_id). and for actor, distinguish human vs service.

Marcus Lin: also: if there’s an “impersonation” flow (support acting as customer admin), include impersonator in actor chain.

Diego Santos: ack. for oncall, actor will be the human via SSO -> admin API token. for private, operator identity is sometimes just “kube user” from kubectl; we’ll capture best-effort.

AuditBot: Reminder: use schema_version in all new audit events and validate against audit-log-schema-v1. Unknown event_type strings will be rejected once ENG-4822 lints are enabled.

Jordan Lee: question: when policy engine pushes down a new routing policy to runtime, do we emit audit in policy engine only, or also in runtime?

Allison Grant: prefer single source of truth for “who changed policy” (policy engine/control plane). runtime can emit operational “policy applied” events, but not duplicate audit unless there’s a separate manual override.

Marcus Lin: +1. avoid dupes. runtime audit should focus on runtime-owned admin actions / overrides.

Diego Santos: cool. action items:
- I’ll write up the sensitive runtime actions list + ownership boundary (policy vs runtime) and drop in #eng-security for taxonomy review
- Logan: once we have event_types, can you add to expected list for coverage dashboard?

Logan Wright: yep, send the final strings + required fields and I’ll add to the “expected events” config.

Hanae Suzuki: please also note which ones must be “guaranteed” vs best-effort. eg enable/disable should probably be “must emit” even if sink is degraded.

Diego Santos: agreed. enable/disable + pool binding + tenant cap overrides = must emit (buffer/retry). tuning-only stays best-effort/telemetry.

Ethan Park: 👍

Marcus Lin: 👍 keep it tight and consistent with the schema. thanks.

- I’ll write up the sensitive runtime actions list + ownership boundary (policy vs runtime) and drop in #eng-security for taxonomy review
- Logan: once we have event_types, can you add to expected list for coverage dashboard?

Logan Wright: yep, send the final strings + required fields and I’ll add to the “expected events” config.

Hanae Suzuki: please also note which ones must be “guaranteed” vs best-effort. eg enable/disable should probably be “must emit” even if sink is degraded.

Diego Santos: agreed. enable/disable + pool binding + tenant cap overrides = must emit (buffer/retry). tuning-only stays best-effort/telemetry.

source.messages:
Nadia Rahman: heads up: cache hit rate signal in metering looks noisy/possibly wrong. In the unit econ views it swings +/-20pp day to day for the same model+region, but latency + GPU-seconds don’t move. Before we bake this into the dashboard “cost drivers” section, can we sanity check what we’re actually measuring?

Caleb Johnson: +1. I pulled yesterday’s hourly aggregates and the cache_hit_rate dropped to ~0.12 for `llama-70b` in us-east for ~3 hours, but request volume + output tokens were normal. That feels like instrumentation drop vs real cache behavior.

Logan Wright: what’s the exact definition we’re using right now? is it (cache_hits / (cache_hits+cache_misses)) at request-level? or token-level? I’ve seen both definitions float around.

Nadia Rahman: current pipeline assumes token-level: `cached_prefill_tokens / total_prefill_tokens` (from runtime stats). But I’m not confident the runtime is emitting `cached_prefill_tokens` consistently across gateways/streaming/tool calls.

Aditya Rao: from Optimize side: we want something that correlates with $/token, not just “hit rate”. If we can estimate “compute avoided” (GPU-seconds saved) or “tokens avoided”, that’s more actionable.

Jada Williams: runtime details: we have two different “caches” people conflate:
- prefix cache (prompt/prefill reuse) => should reduce prefill compute
- KV reuse within a session / continuation => shows up differently (and can be near-100% for some chat patterns)
Our metric today is basically prefix cache only, but the naming is ambiguous.

Allison Grant: also: we’re sampling runtime metrics in a couple places. If the cache counters are in a different telemetry stream than token counters, you can get temporary skew (dropouts look like “hit rate fell off a cliff”).

Sean Gallagher: +1. We saw similar with other runtime counters last quarter. If the numerator is missing but denominator isn’t, you’ll alarm on “hit rate regression” when it’s just missing fields.

Mei Lin: I can confirm in the warehouse: in those 3 hours `cached_prefill_tokens` is NULL for a bunch of rows (by route), but `prefill_tokens` is populated. Our view is coercing NULL to 0 => hit rate tanks.

Caleb Johnson: ah, that explains the cliff. We should treat NULL as “unknown” and exclude from hit rate, or have a coverage indicator.

Dylan Brooks: agree. For exec/finance we should not surface a driver metric that can be “0 because missing data”. Need guardrails.

Nadia Rahman: ok, so action items:
1) fix aggregation semantics: NULL => unknown; compute hit_rate only when both fields present; add `cache_signal_coverage`.
2) clarify definitions in the metric dictionary: prefix cache vs session KV reuse.
But I still want to answer: how do we attribute cache “savings” to blended $/1M output tokens?

Aditya Rao: mental model: blended $/1M output tokens is essentially (cost / output_tokens). Cache reduces compute for a given output token volume. The best “savings” metric would be something like (gpu_seconds_saved / output_tokens) or (prefill_gpu_seconds_saved per output token).

Jada Williams: we can’t currently emit “gpu_seconds_saved” directly. But we can emit:
- prefill_tokens_total
- prefill_tokens_cached
- prefill_ms_total (already have)
Then approximate “ms avoided” as (prefill_ms_per_token_baseline * cached_tokens). Problem: baseline depends on model, seq len, quant, hw.

Quentin Harris: baseline will vary by model version + quant profile. If we try to infer savings, we need to join on model_version + quantization_profile, otherwise it’s garbage.

Logan Wright: for v1 dashboard, maybe don’t compute $ savings from cache. Instead show cache hit rate + prefill/decode ratio + batching utilization as *explanatory signals*, and keep cost metric separate.

Caleb Johnson: but the PRD explicitly wants “contributing signals” to be interpretable. If cache hit rate goes up, people will assume cost should go down. We need guidance like: “cache hit rate affects prefill; if your workload is decode-dominant, hit rate won’t move blended cost much.”

Nadia Rahman: exactly. We can pair it with `prefill_to_total_compute_ratio` (or proxy). Is there an existing runtime metric for prefill vs decode time split?

Jada Williams: we have `prefill_ms` and `decode_ms` in the per-request stats, but they’re not reliably emitted on the streaming path (ironic given this thread). Also tool calling can break the accounting.

Allison Grant: if we’re going to rely on these signals, we need:
- explicit schema contract
- “required fields” completeness checks
- a way to detect silent regressions (canary)
Otherwise the dashboard will become a blame magnet.

Sean Gallagher: +1. For oncall: simplest is alert on coverage dropping below X% for required dimensions/fields. Cache signals can be “best effort” but must be labeled as such.

Dylan Brooks: can we converge on what we’ll ship for v1?
My proposal:
- Show cache hit rate as token-level prefix cache hit rate.
- Show a “coverage badge” (% requests w/ cache counters present).
- Add tooltip: only impacts prefill; cost sensitivity depends on prefill share.
- Do NOT show “$ saved” until we have a defensible model.

Aditya Rao: works for me. Optimize can still use the signals to prioritize work, but we won’t over-claim.

Mei Lin: I’ll implement the NULL-handling + coverage metric in the view. We can define:
`cache_counter_coverage = requests_with_cached_prefill_tokens / total_requests`
(or token-weighted coverage if preferred).

Logan Wright: request-weighted is fine for coverage as long as we’re consistent. Token-weighted might hide missing on high-volume short requests.

Nadia Rahman: on runtime side, can we fix the streaming emission? It sounds like `cached_prefill_tokens` + `prefill_ms/decode_ms` aren’t always present.

Jada Williams: I can take a look. I suspect the streaming code path bypasses the “finalize stats” block when the client disconnects early. That would drop cache counters and time split. If that’s true, we need to flush partial stats on stream close.

Caleb Johnson: can we add a simple parity test like: for a fixed prompt that should be cacheable, run N requests and ensure cached_prefill_tokens increases on request 2+ across streaming and non-streaming.

Allison Grant: and wire that into perf-canary. It’s exactly the kind of thing that regresses quietly.

redwood-bot: Reminder: please file follow-ups in Linear under Unit Economics > Runtime Signals.

Nadia Rahman: filed:
- runtime: “streaming path emits cache + prefill/decode counters consistently (incl early disconnect)”
- analytics: “cache hit rate NULL semantics + coverage metric + dashboard tooltip copy”

Sean Gallagher: once coverage is in place, I’ll add an alert threshold like “cache_counter_coverage < 0.95 for 2 consecutive hours” to eng-runtime/eng-sre (non-paging at first).

Dylan Brooks: cool. Also please make sure the dashboard doesn’t let folks drill down to anything customer-identifying when looking at cache. Aggregation only.

Nadia Rahman: yep. This will be at model/region/tier only.

Aditya Rao: last ask: can we standardize naming? “prefix_cache_hit_rate” not “cache_hit_rate”. Otherwise people will assume it includes KV reuse and request-level caching.

Quentin Harris: +1. Also include quant_profile dimension where available; cache behavior can change with context window defaults and batching.

Nadia Rahman: agreed. I’ll update the data dictionary draft + ping Alicia (design) for label/tooltip updates.

source.text:
Noah Patel: starting a thread on runtime side for safe apply/rollback. main questions: idempotency semantics, worker cache invalidation, and what we do under partial rollout / mixed state.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. esp worried about KV/prefix cache. if config toggles caching/batching knobs, a rollback could leave workers with stale cache assumptions.

Sofia Ivanova: also the batching heuristics changes are stateful-ish (scheduler params). we need to be clear whether “apply v123” is best-effort vs guaranteed across all workers before controller moves on.

Benji Okafor: from control plane side, rollout controller will treat runtime as eventually consistent but needs a strong signal: “applied checkpoint reached for shard X” + ability to retry safely.

Noah Patel: proposal: runtime applier is idempotent per (scope, config_version). Think of it as:
- input: `tenant_id`, `model`, `region`, `config_version_id`, `effective_config_hash`
- worker stores `last_applied_version_id` + `last_applied_hash` (persisted locally + exported metric)
- if same version/hash comes in again => no-op
- if newer version => apply steps with checkpoints

Omar El-Sayed: where do we store that on the worker? local disk? in-memory only isn’t safe under restarts.

Noah Patel: lightweight local state file (e.g., `/var/lib/redwood/runtime/opt_cfg_state.json`) or in our existing worker metadata store. doesn’t have to be perfect, but we want to avoid thrash on rolling restarts.

Sean Gallagher: from SRE pov, idempotency also matters when control plane retries due to network flakes. we see a lot of duplicate “apply” calls in incidents.

Sofia Ivanova: what’s the “apply steps with checkpoints” look like? esp for batching heuristics.

Noah Patel: rough sequence per worker:
1) fetch config blob (by version) from config service via control plane cache
2) validate minimum compatibility (runtime version, model arch constraints) — if fail, report `apply_status=FAILED_COMPAT`
3) stage new config into “pending” slot (don’t switch traffic)
4) drain in-flight batches for that model worker (bounded wait, e.g., 2s)
5) switch scheduler params + caching toggles
6) flush/namespace caches as needed
7) mark applied + emit metric + log/audit correlation id

Omar El-Sayed: cache invalidation: I’d prefer namespacing rather than full flush if possible. if config introduces new cache key format we can version the namespace by config_version.

Noah Patel: yep. for prefix cache we can include `opt_cfg_epoch` in the key prefix. then rollback just uses previous epoch and ignores “new” entries. memory will still be used though.

Omar El-Sayed: memory leak risk if we keep old epochs around. can we GC older epochs? maybe keep last 2 versions per (tenant, model)?

Noah Patel: GC policy: maintain `active_epoch` and `previous_epoch`; anything else eligible for eviction. on rollback, controller sets version pointer back, so `previous_epoch` flips. we can keep a small LRU per epoch.

Allison Grant: metrics tagging: please please tag runtime metrics with `config_version` (or epoch) so gates can see mixed states. if a canary is 10% workers on v124, we need that dimension.

Noah Patel: agreed. we’ll emit:
- `runtime_opt_cfg_applied{tenant,model,region,config_version}` gauge=1 on worker
- request path: add `config_version` label on latency/error metrics (careful cardinality but config_version should be low)

Sean Gallagher: cardinality: tenant+model+region already big. adding version might explode if customers churn versions. any cap?

Allison Grant: for gates we only need it during rollout window. we can do it as an annotation/event too, but we still need *some* way to separate canary vs baseline.

Noah Patel: compromise: worker emits `runtime_opt_cfg_active_version` as an info metric (Prometheus “info” style) and request metrics get `opt_cfg_bucket` = {baseline,canary} during rollout. control plane defines which workers are canary.

Benji Okafor: we can pass “rollout cohort id” to runtime with the apply call. then runtime can label request metrics with cohort rather than exact version.

Sofia Ivanova: but how does runtime know for a request which cohort it is? the worker itself is in a cohort, so ok.

Noah Patel: yep, per-worker cohort label.

Omar El-Sayed: partial rollout handling: what if we have mixed config within a single multi-GPU node? like 8 workers and only 2 are canary. fine, but for shared caches (if any) we must ensure isolation.

Noah Patel: we don’t have truly shared caches across workers today (each worker has its own). except some node-level artifacts like compiled kernels cache. that one shouldn’t depend on opt cfg.

Omar El-Sayed: except quantization profiles could change kernel selection / weights formats. that *does* touch compiled artifacts and weight blobs.

Noah Patel: good call. for quant profiles we treat it as “model artifact variant” and load weights under a versioned path. we should not hot-swap quant weights in-place without a drain.

Sofia Ivanova: so quant profile apply might require a full model reload, which is slow. how does canary controller treat that? timeouts?

Benji Okafor: controller will support per-change “expected apply duration” and will pause if apply doesn’t converge within window. but runtime needs to report progress (DOWNLOADING, LOADING, WARMING).

Noah Patel: we’ll add apply statuses:
- PENDING
- DOWNLOADING_ARTIFACTS
- LOADING_MODEL
- WARMING
- APPLIED
- FAILED (with reason)

Sean Gallagher: + include `attempt_id` and `request_id` from control plane in logs. makes debugging easier.

Noah Patel: partial apply + rollback race: scenario:
- rollout to 10% starts applying v124
- gate fails, rollback to v123 triggered
- some workers still mid-apply v124
Need deterministic outcome: rollback wins.

Connor O'Brien: release eng note: please define “rollback wins” semantics precisely; we’ll need it in release checklist and gameday.

Noah Patel: semantics: each apply/rollback call carries a monotonically increasing `desired_generation` per scope. worker only applies commands with generation >= current. if rollback is newer generation, any in-progress apply should abort at checkpoint boundary.

Omar El-Sayed: abort mid-flight is tricky. if we’re in the middle of reloading model weights, we can’t partially revert. safest is: finish to a safe point then apply rollback.

Noah Patel: right: we don’t hard-kill. we set `abort_requested=true` and at the next checkpoint we stop advancing and transition to “needs_reconcile”; then reconcile loop drives toward newest desired generation.

Sofia Ivanova: what are checkpoints for batching/caching changes? those can be hot-swapped quickly. quant reload is the long one.

Noah Patel: checkpoint boundaries:
- before switching scheduler params
- before swapping cache epoch
- before starting model reload
- after model reload
So rollback could prevent starting reload if it arrives early.

Benji Okafor: controller side will avoid sending conflicting commands too frequently, but we can’t rely on that.

Sean Gallagher: also need a safeguard against thrashing: if someone repeatedly applies/rollbacks, workers will churn and SLOs go sideways.

Noah Patel: we can add a per-scope cooldown on runtime side (e.g., don’t start another expensive transition within 60s unless `force=true` from oncall). control plane likely also has a cooldown.

Allison Grant: gating signals during partial rollout: we need to know if errors/latency are coming from canary only. cohort tag solves.

preview:
Noah Patel: starting a thread on runtime side for safe apply/rollback. main questions: idempotency semantics, worker cache invalidation, and what we do under partial rollout / mixed state.

Omar El-Sayed: +1. esp worried about KV/prefix cache. if config toggles caching/batching knobs, a rollback could leave workers with stale cache assumptions.

Sofia Ivanova: also the batching heuristics changes are stateful-ish (scheduler params). we need to be clear whether “apply v123” is best-effort vs guaranteed across all workers before controller moves on.

Benji Okafor: from control plane side, rollout controller will treat runtime as eventually consistent but needs a strong signal: “applied checkpoint reached for shard X” + ability to retry safely.

Noah Patel: proposal: runtime applier is idempotent per (scope, config_version). Think of it as:
- input: `tenant_id`, `model`, `region`, `config_version_id`, `effective_config_hash`
- worker stores `last_applied_version_id` + `last_applied_hash` (persisted locally + exported metric)
- if same version/hash comes in again => no-op
- if newer version => apply steps with checkpoints

Omar El-Sayed: where do we store that on the worker? local disk? in-memory only isn’t safe under restarts.

Noah Patel: lightweight local state file (e.g., `/var/lib/redwood/runtime/opt_cfg_state.json`) or in our existing worker metadata store. doesn’t have to be perfect, but we want to avoid thrash on rolling restarts.

Sean Gallagher: from SRE pov, idempotency also matters when control plane retries due to network flakes. we see a lot of duplicate “apply” calls in incidents.

Sofia Ivanova: what’s the “apply steps with checkpoints” look like? esp for batching heuristics.

Noah Patel: rough sequence per worker:
1) fetch config blob (by version) from config service via control plane cache
2) validate minimum compatibility (runtime version, model arch constraints) — if fail, report `apply_status=FAILED_COMPAT`
3) stage new config into “pending” slot (don’t switch traffic)
4) drain in-flight batches for that model worker (bounded wait, e.g., 2s)
5) switch scheduler params + caching toggles
6) flush/namespace caches as needed
7) mark applied + emit metric + log/audit correlation id

Omar El-Sayed: cache invalidation: I’d prefer namespacing rather than full flush if possible. if config introduces new cache key format we can version the namespace by config_version.

Noah Patel: yep. for prefix cache we can include `opt_cfg_epoch` in the key prefix. then rollback just uses previous epoch and ignores “new” entries. memory will still be used though.

Omar El-Sayed: memory leak risk if we keep old epochs around. can we GC older epochs? maybe keep last 2 versions per (tenant, model)?

Noah Patel: GC policy: maintain `active_epoch` and `previous_epoch`; anything else eligible for eviction. on rollback, controller sets version pointer back, so `previous_epoch` flips. we can keep a small LRU per epoch.

Allison Grant: metrics tagging: please please tag runtime metrics with `config_version` (or epoch) so gates can see mixed states. if a canary is 10% workers on v124, we need that dimension.

Noah Patel: agreed. we’ll emit:
- `runtime_opt_cfg_applied{tenant,model,region,config_version}` gauge=1 on worker
- request path: add `config_version` label on latency/error metrics (careful cardinality but config_version should be low)

Sean Gallagher: cardinality: tenant+model+region already big. adding version might explode if customers churn versions. any cap?

Allison Grant: for gates we only need it during rollout window. we can do it as an annotation/event too, but we still need *some* way to separate canary vs baseline.

Noah Patel: compromise: worker emits `runtime_opt_cfg_active_version` as an info metric (Prometheus “info” style) and request metrics get `opt_cfg_bucket` = {baseline,canary} during rollout. control plane defines which workers are canary.

Benji Okafor: we can pass “rollout cohort id” to runtime with the apply call. then runtime can label request metrics with cohort rather than exact version.

Sofia Ivanova: but how does runtime know for a request which cohort it is? the worker itself is in a cohort, so ok.

Noah Patel: yep, per-worker cohort label.

Omar El-Sayed: partial rollout handling: what if we have mixed config within a single multi-GPU node? like 8 workers and only 2 are canary. fine, but for shared caches (if any) we must ensure isolation.

Noah Patel: we don’t have truly shared caches across workers today (each worker has its own). except some node-level artifacts like compiled kernels cache. that one shouldn’t depend on opt cfg.

Omar El-Sayed: except quantization profiles could change kernel selection / weights formats. that *does* touch compiled artifacts and weight blobs.

Noah Patel: good call. for quant profiles we treat it as “model artifact variant” and load weights under a versioned path. we should not hot-swap quant weights in-place without a drain.

Sofia Ivanova: so quant profile apply might require a full model reload, which is slow. how does canary controller treat that? timeouts?

Benji Okafor: controller will support per-change “expected apply duration” and will pause if apply doesn’t converge within window. but runtime needs to report progress (DOWNLOADING, LOADING, WARMING).

Noah Patel: we’ll add apply statuses:
- PENDING
- DOWNLOADING_ARTIFACTS
- LOADING_MODEL
- WARMING
- APPLIED
- FAILED (with reason)

Sean Gallagher: + include `attempt_id` and `request_id` from control plane in logs. makes debugging easier.

Noah Patel: partial apply + rollback race: scenario:
- rollout to 10% starts applying v124
- gate fails, rollback to v123 triggered
- some workers still mid-apply v124
Need deterministic outcome: rollback wins.

Connor O'Brien: release eng note: please define “rollback wins” semantics precisely; we’ll need it in release checklist and gameday.

Noah Patel: semantics: each apply/rollback call carries a monotonically increasing `desired_generation` per scope. worker only applies commands with generation >= current. if rollback is newer generation, any in-progress apply should abort at checkpoint boundary.

Omar El-Sayed: abort mid-flight is tricky. if we’re in the middle of reloading model weights, we can’t partially revert. safest is: finish to a safe point then apply rollback.

Noah Patel: right: we don’t hard-kill. we set `abort_requested=true` and at the next checkpoint we stop advancing and transition to “needs_reconcile”; then reconcile loop drives toward newest desired generation.

Sofia Ivanova: what are checkpoints for batching/caching changes? those can be hot-swapped quickly. quant reload is the long one.

Noah Patel: checkpoint boundaries:
- before switching scheduler params
- before swapping cache epoch
- before starting model reload
- after model reload
So rollback could prevent starting reload if it arrives early.

Benji Okafor: controller side will avoid sending conflicting commands too frequently, but we can’t rely on that.

Sean Gallagher: also need a safeguard against thrashing: if someone repeatedly applies/rollbacks, workers will churn and SLOs go sideways.

Noah Patel: we can add a per-scope cooldown on runtime side (e.g., don’t start another expensive transition within 60s unless `force=true` from oncall). control plane likely also has a cooldown.

Noah Patel: if caching disabled, we can stop reads/writes but optionally schedule background eviction. no need for synchronous flush.

Noah Patel: we’ll drain in-flight then switch; should be low impact but we should measure. canary gating should allow a short “settle window” after apply before evaluating p95/p99.

Benji Okafor: ADR draft for gates includes settle time + minimum sample size. good.

Connor O'Brien: timeline wise, can you get the generation/checkpoint protocol into the next runtime train? we’re trying to align with console rollout.

Noah Patel: yes if we keep it minimal: generation integer per scope, status reporting, epoch-based cache key. quant reload statuses may come in follow-up.

source.text:
Ava Chen: Nightly perf job artifacts size spiked — nsys captures for the KV-heavy smoke runs are ~1.6GB each and uploads are timing out. Seen this on the new kvcache evictions tests. Anyone else?
Samir Patel: Yup. We capture a lot (full cuda context + nvtx + memory). We should trim capture scope and only collect full nsys when the run looks anomalous. :eyes:
Liam O'Neill: What exactly is CI running atm? can you paste the command?
Ava Chen: Current CI snippet (from circle job):
```bash
nsys profile --output ${ARTIFACT} --trace=cuda,nvtx --force-overwrite 
  --stats=true 
  --stop-on-exit 
  python run_kvcache_smoke.py --iters 100
```
Samir Patel: Ok. A few levers: 1) restrict trace range (e.g. --capture-range=nvtx) 2) disable heavy memory tracking 3) use nsys --compress or switch to perfetto for lightweight timeline. Also consider sampling only when kv miss rate > threshold.
Julie Ramos: We tried compressing but the diff dashboard can't meaningfully diff compressed blobs. We need a strategy: keep small daily artifacts for diffs and keep one raw artifact per N nights for deep inspection.
perf-ci-bot: perf-ci-bot: Proposal: store full nsys trace for 1/7 nightly runs (weekly baseline) and lightweight perfetto+kernel-timeline JSON for daily runs. Upload prefix: s3://redwood-perf/nightly/1732109876/ (example).
Samir Patel: Also noticed kernel-selection noise — tiny ordering differences in kernels generate huge binary diffs. Suggest we canonicalize kernel timeline before computing diffs: group by NVTX op id and sort kernels by start time within op, then normalize timestamps to op-relative.
Ava Chen: Can we make the sampler only capture full nsys when KV cache miss ratio > 5%? That would focus storage on problematic runs.
Liam O'Neill: We already emit metrics.csv from runs with fields: total_requests, cache_hits, cache_misses. We can compute kv_miss_ratio = cache_misses / total_requests and gate capture. Proposed change to CI sampler: if kv_miss_ratio > ${THRESHOLD} -> full nsys, else perfetto small trace.
Liam O'Neill: Pseudocode for tools/trace_sampler.py:
```python
metrics = read_csv('metrics.csv')
miss_ratio = metrics['cache_misses'] / metrics['total_requests']
if miss_ratio > threshold:
    run('nsys profile --output full.ns --trace=cuda,nvtx --compress')
else:
    run('python -m perfetto_cli --out lightweight.pb --timeline')
```
Samir Patel: Additions: (a) autosplit large nsys artifacts with --max-size flag or do on-the-fly compression, (b) multipart S3 upload + retries in uploader to avoid timeouts.
Julie Ramos: Retention proposal: full raw nsys -> 30 days, lightweight perfetto + normalized JSON -> 90 days, diff artifacts -> 365 days. Update pruning job accordingly.
Ava Chen: Can someone own dashboard ingest changes? need to parse both nsys and perfetto, and apply kernel timeline normalization before diffing. Dashboard prototype: http://dashboards.internal/perf/kvtrace (private).
Liam O'Neill: I'll take dashboard parsing + kernel normalization. Will add a normalization step that maps kernels to op NVTX ids and produces a stable JSON for diffs.
perf-ci-bot: perf-ci-bot: Created tasks: PERF-4321 trace_sampler threshold & compression, PERF-4322 dashboard parser + normalization, PERF-4323 retention & pruning job update.
Julie Ramos: One more: add an NVTX annotation hook in our test harness so traces contain consistent op labels (module.function:op) — helps the grouping.
Ava Chen: Great plan. I'll open the PR for trace_sampler with a canary rollout to nightly for 3 runs, then flip on the sampler globally if stable. Thanks all. :thumbsup:

Ava Chen: Nightly perf job artifacts size spiked — nsys captures for the KV-heavy smoke runs are ~1.6GB each and uploads are timing out. Seen this on the new kvcache evictions tests. Anyone else?
Samir Patel: Yup. We capture a lot (full cuda context + nvtx + memory). We should trim capture scope and only collect full nsys when the run looks anomalous. :eyes:
Liam O'Neill: What exactly is CI running atm? can you paste the command?
Ava Chen: Current CI snippet (from circle job):
```bash
nsys profile --output ${ARTIFACT} --trace=cuda,nvtx --force-overwrite 
  --stats=true 
  --stop-on-exit 
  python run_kvcache_smoke.py --iters 100
```
Samir Patel: Ok. A few levers: 1) restrict trace range (e.g. --capture-range=nvtx) 2) disable heavy memory tracking 3) use nsys --compress or switch to perfetto for lightweight timeline. Also consider sampling only when kv miss rate > threshold.
Julie Ramos: We tried compressing but the diff dashboard can't meaningfully diff compressed blobs. We need a strategy: keep small daily artifacts for diffs and keep one raw artifact per N nights for deep inspection.
perf-ci-bot: perf-ci-bot: Proposal: store full nsys trace for 1/7 nightly runs (weekly baseline) and lightweight perfetto+kernel-timeline JSON for daily runs. Upload prefix: s3://redwood-perf/nightly/1732109876/ (example).
Samir Patel: Also noticed kernel-selection noise — tiny ordering differences in kernels generate huge binary diffs. Suggest we canonicalize kernel timeline before computing diffs: group by NVTX op id and sort kernels by start time within op, then normalize timestamps to op-relative.
Ava Chen: Can we make the sampler only capture full nsys when KV cache miss ratio > 5%? That would focus storage on problematic runs.
Liam O'Neill: We already emit metrics.csv from runs with fields: total_requests, cache_hits, cache_misses. We can compute kv_miss_ratio = cache_misses / total_requests and gate capture. Proposed change to CI sampler: if kv_miss_ratio > ${THRESHOLD} -> full nsys, else perfetto small trace.
Liam O'Neill: Pseudocode for tools/trace_sampler.py:
```python
metrics = read_csv('metrics.csv')
miss_ratio = metrics['cache_misses'] / metrics['total_requests']
if miss_ratio > threshold:
    run('nsys profile --output full.ns --trace=cuda,nvtx --compress')
else:
    run('python -m perfetto_cli --out lightweight.pb --timeline')
```
Samir Patel: Additions: (a) autosplit large nsys artifacts with --max-size flag or do on-the-fly compression, (b) multipart S3 upload + retries in uploader to avoid timeouts.
Julie Ramos: Retention proposal: full raw nsys -> 30 days, lightweight perfetto + normalized JSON -> 90 days, diff artifacts -> 365 days. Update pruning job accordingly.

Ava Chen: Great plan. I'll open the PR for trace_sampler with a canary rollout to nightly for 3 runs, then flip on the sampler globally if stable. Thanks all. :thumbsup:

Maria Chen: Heads-up — planning a gated rollout for kernel-quant v3.2 tonight in us-west (change window 02:00-03:00 PT). DeployBot will run preflight smoke + perf canary. Freeze engine non-essential merges at 00:30 PT. Please ack if you can watch metrics and own rollback if needed. Release notes draft: https://redwood.internal/releases/kernel-quant/v3.2 :memo:

Kevin Liu: ack. I'll monitor p50/p95, error-rate, and GPU temp dashboards. Posting links in a bit.

DeployBot: Preflight queued: [smoke, kv-cache-sanity, perf-runner]. Expected checks ~10m. Will report back.

Jamie Ortiz: Quick question — what are our rollback thresholds? Proposing: p95 latency +30ms sustained for 5m OR error-rate >0.5% sustained for 5m OR kv-miss-rate spike >2x baseline. If any hit -> immediate roll to v3.1 pin.

Akshay Rao: Adding metrics to the watchlist: kv-miss-rate, batching-latency, gpu-temp, warmstart-throttle, host-spare-capacity. Also watch model-exact-match on our small eval set (top-1 diffs).

Priya Singh: I can own rollbacks tonight (on call 01:30-04:00 PT). I'll standby for canary->50% decision. Leave the rollback playbook to me if alerts fire.

Kevin Liu: Note: this build uses matmul-v2 kernels and quant profile B. If we see small-prompt inconsistency (embedding/eval regress), fallback to matmul-v1 shader should be allowed. Confirming auto-variant fallback is configured for region us-west.

SRE-Bot: Incident runbook link: https://redwood.internal/runbooks/kernel-quant-rollback. Short checklist included: reproduce on staging -> pin traffic -> escalate to oncall -> hotpatch if needed.

Maria Chen: Ops checklist (short):
- freeze merges at 00:30 PT
- run `./scripts/preflight.sh --canary` (done by DeployBot)
- ensure perf-runner CI passed within last 2 hrs
- pin TTL configured to 30m for emergency rollbacks

Maria Chen: Release notes snippet to use in marketplace/console: "kernel-quant v3.2 — improved matmul throughput, reduced memory copy for KV cache, updated quant profile B for lower variance at long sequences." Keep it brief.

DeployBot: Staging smoke passed. Canary targets: initial 10 hosts, then 25%, then 50%, then full in 10m windows. Canary started.

Ethan Park: FYI saw an earlier perf-runner OOM during a stress harness on a staging host — attached short repro log: `oom-kernel-quant.txt`. I isolated the container and removed it from the staging canary.

Maria Chen: Thanks Ethan — removed that instance from canary target group. Good catch.

DeployBot: Canary rollout event: started rollout phase at ts 1712106000. Rolling: 10% -> 25% -> 50% with 10m interstitial waits.

Jamie Ortiz: Emergency pin command ready if warmstart regress shows up:```/deploybot pin region=us-west engine=kernel-quant v=3.1 ttl=30m -reason "rollback warmstart regress"```

Priya Singh: Dashboards are up in #eng-runtime: links to kv-miss, p95, error-rate. I'll post annotated baseline numbers for quick eyeball.

Kevin Liu: Also adding a quick curl check for canaries: ```curl -sS -H "Authorization: Bearer $CANARY_TOKEN" https://canary.us-west.redwood/api/v1/health | jq .latency_ms``` — drop me any anomalies.

Maria Chen: If we have no blocking signals by 1712106600 we'll proceed to full rollout. Please react :thumbsup: if you saw this and are good to go.

DeployBot: Canary completed 50% stage — no auto-failures detected in the automated checks. Next window scheduled.

Priya Singh: Oncall note: I'll escalate to SRE if we see any error-rate spikes >0.2% in the next 20m.

Maria Chen: Thanks everyone. If anything urgent ping @Priya or @Jamie directly. Will post final rollout completion once done. :rocket:
